questions.question: Which transmission mode allows data to flow in only one direction, with no return path?
questions.answer: Simplex
questions.explanation: Simplex is unidirectional (e.g., broadcast TV, keyboard to host). Half-duplex allows both directions but not simultaneously (walkie-talkies). Full-duplex allows simultaneous bidirectional flow (modern Ethernet). Multiplex is not a transmission mode; it is a technique for combining multiple signals on one medium.

questions.question: The Nyquist theorem for a noiseless channel states the maximum bit rate equals which expression, where B is bandwidth and L is the number of signal levels?
questions.answer: 2 B log2(L)
questions.explanation: Nyquist's formula for a noiseless channel is C = 2B·log2(L), giving the theoretical upper bound in the absence of noise. B·log2(1+SNR) is Shannon's capacity for a noisy channel. B/L and 2B·L are not standard capacity results.

questions.question: What is the key difference between bit rate and baud rate?
options: Bit rate counts bits per second; baud rate counts signal-level changes per second
Bit rate is measured in hertz; baud rate in bits per second
Baud rate applies only to analog signals; bit rate only to digital
questions.answer: Bit rate counts bits per second; baud rate counts signal-level changes per second
questions.explanation: Bit rate is information per second; baud (symbol) rate is symbols (signaling elements) per second. Bit rate = baud rate × bits-per-symbol, so they are equal only when each symbol carries exactly one bit. The other options confuse units or conflate modulation with signal domain.

questions.question: Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM) works by:
options: Assigning each channel a distinct frequency band within the shared spectrum
Assigning each channel a time slot in a repeating frame
Spreading each signal with a unique code over the whole band
Sending each channel on a different optical wavelength in the same fiber
questions.answer: Assigning each channel a distinct frequency band within the shared spectrum
questions.explanation: FDM partitions the available spectrum into non-overlapping frequency bands. TDM assigns time slots. CDMA spreads signals with orthogonal codes. WDM is essentially FDM applied to optical wavelengths and is considered a separate technique at that layer.

questions.question: In NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) line coding, a binary 1 and 0 are represented by:
options: Two different voltage levels that are held for the whole bit period with no return to zero mid-bit
A transition at the middle of the bit period
A pulse that returns to zero halfway through each bit
questions.answer: Two different voltage levels that are held for the whole bit period with no return to zero mid-bit
questions.explanation: NRZ uses two steady voltage levels for the entire bit interval and never returns to zero mid-bit, which is why it has poor clock recovery on long runs of identical bits. A mid-bit transition describes Manchester. A return-to-zero pulse describes RZ. Orthogonal codes describe CDMA, not a line code.

questions.question: Synchronous Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM) allocates:
options: A fixed time slot to each source, whether or not it has data to send
Time slots only to sources that currently have data
A frequency band to each source
A spreading code to each source
questions.answer: A fixed time slot to each source, whether or not it has data to send
questions.explanation: Synchronous TDM pre-assigns a slot per source per frame, so idle sources waste their slot. Statistical (asynchronous) TDM skips idle sources and labels slots with a source identifier. Frequency bands are FDM; spreading codes are CDMA.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of guided transmission media?
questions.answer: Unshielded twisted pair (UTP)
questions.explanation: Guided media confine signals to a physical conductor or fiber: UTP, STP, coax, fiber. Microwave, satellite, and infrared free-space are unguided (wireless) media since the signal propagates through free space.

questions.question: Attenuation refers to:
options: Loss of signal energy as it travels through a medium
Unwanted coupling of a signal from one conductor to another
Variation of propagation delay with frequency
Random thermal variations added to the signal
questions.answer: Loss of signal energy as it travels through a medium
questions.explanation: Attenuation is power loss with distance, usually expressed in dB. Crosstalk is coupling between conductors. Distortion (specifically dispersion in this form) is frequency-dependent delay. Random thermal fluctuations are thermal noise.

questions.question: Single-mode fiber (SMF) is generally preferred over multimode fiber (MMF) for long-distance links because SMF:
options: Has a smaller core that supports only one propagation mode, reducing modal dispersion
Uses LEDs instead of lasers and is therefore cheaper
Carries multiple wavelengths in separate modes
Uses an electrical conductor rather than glass
questions.answer: Has a smaller core that supports only one propagation mode, reducing modal dispersion
questions.explanation: SMF's ~8–10 μm core allows effectively one guided mode, eliminating modal dispersion and enabling long-reach high-rate links. MMF (50/62.5 μm) supports many modes and is used for short reach with cheaper LEDs/VCSELs. SMF uses narrow-linewidth lasers, not LEDs, and fiber is glass, not metal.

questions.question: How many bits are carried per symbol in 16-QAM?
questions.explanation: M-QAM carries log2(M) bits per symbol, so 16-QAM carries log2(16)=4 bits/symbol. 2 would be QPSK (4-QAM). 8 would be 256-QAM. 16 is the constellation size, not the bits per symbol.

questions.question: Asynchronous transmission frames each character with:
options: A start bit and one or more stop bits
questions.answer: A start bit and one or more stop bits
questions.explanation: In asynchronous transmission the receiver resynchronizes per character using a start bit (logic 0) and one or more stop bits (logic 1). 8B/10B, preambles, and CRCs are used in synchronous framed protocols such as Ethernet/Gigabit links, not character-level async transmission.

questions.question: Which property distinguishes an analog signal from a digital signal?
options: Analog signals vary continuously; digital signals take on discrete values
Analog signals are always faster than digital
Analog signals cannot be transmitted on copper
questions.answer: Analog signals vary continuously; digital signals take on discrete values
questions.explanation: By definition analog amplitudes form a continuum while digital signals use a finite discrete alphabet (typically 2 levels). Speed comparisons depend on technology, not type. Digital signals routinely carry digitized voice (PCM), and analog signals travel on copper daily.

questions.question: The primary purpose of multiplexing is to:
options: Share a single high-capacity link among multiple signals
questions.answer: Share a single high-capacity link among multiple signals
questions.explanation: Multiplexing combines multiple channels onto one physical medium to use capacity efficiently. Error detection is handled by CRCs/checksums, secrecy by cryptography, and analog-to-digital conversion by sampling and quantization (e.g., PCM).

questions.question: Which noise type is caused by the thermal agitation of electrons in a conductor and is present in any medium above absolute zero?
questions.answer: Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise
questions.explanation: Thermal noise, also called Johnson–Nyquist noise, comes from random electron motion and scales with temperature and bandwidth (kTB). Crosstalk arises from coupling between conductors, impulse noise from sudden spikes (e.g., switching transients), and intermodulation noise from nonlinear mixing of signals.

questions.question: A data rate expressed as 'Mbps' means:
questions.answer: Megabits per second (10^6 bits/s)
questions.explanation: Lowercase 'b' in Mbps denotes bits per second; uppercase 'B' (MBps) denotes bytes per second, an 8× difference. MHz per symbol is a bandwidth-to-baud relation, and packets per second is pps, not Mbps.

questions.question: A voice channel has bandwidth 4 kHz and signal-to-noise ratio of 30 dB. Using Shannon's formula, what is the approximate channel capacity?
questions.answer: About 40 kbps
questions.explanation: 30 dB ≈ SNR ratio of 1000. C = B·log2(1+SNR) = 4000·log2(1001) ≈ 4000·9.97 ≈ 39.9 kbps. 12 kbps would correspond to about 8 dB SNR, 120 kbps to roughly 90 dB, and 4 kbps is merely the bandwidth.

questions.question: On a noiseless 3 kHz channel using 4 signal levels, the maximum bit rate by Nyquist's theorem is:
questions.answer: 12 kbps
questions.explanation: C = 2B·log2(L) = 2·3000·log2(4) = 6000·2 = 12000 bps = 12 kbps. 6 kbps would be L=2, 3 kbps only matches the bandwidth, and 24 kbps would require L=16 or wider bandwidth.

questions.question: Manchester encoding at a user bit rate of 10 Mbps requires a signaling (baud) rate of:
questions.answer: 20 Mbaud
questions.explanation: Manchester always has a mid-bit transition, so every bit uses two signal elements. Baud rate therefore equals 2× the bit rate (20 Mbaud for 10 Mbps). 10 or 5 Mbaud would apply to codes with one or half symbol per bit; 2.5 Mbaud is unrelated.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the 4B/5B block code used in 100BASE-FX Fast Ethernet?
options: Guarantee frequent transitions so the receiver can recover a clock, while avoiding long runs of zeros
Provide error correction capable of fixing single-bit errors
Compress the payload to reduce bandwidth usage
questions.answer: Guarantee frequent transitions so the receiver can recover a clock, while avoiding long runs of zeros
questions.explanation: 4B/5B maps each 4-bit nibble to a 5-bit code word chosen so that no more than three consecutive zeros appear, supporting clock recovery when transmitted as NRZI. It adds no encryption, correction, or compression—the 25% overhead is pure line-coding tax.

questions.question: Four 1 Mbps channels are combined using synchronous TDM with a 1-byte slot per channel. If framing overhead is negligible, the aggregate link data rate must be at least:
questions.answer: 4 Mbps
questions.explanation: Synchronous TDM preserves each source's full rate, so the trunk must carry the sum: 4 × 1 Mbps = 4 Mbps. 1 Mbps would only carry one source, 2 Mbps is insufficient, and 8 Mbps implies double the needed capacity.

questions.question: Compared with synchronous TDM, statistical TDM:
options: Uses bandwidth more efficiently when sources are bursty because unused slots are skipped, but requires per-slot addressing
Always provides lower latency regardless of load
Eliminates the need for a framing bit pattern
Is limited to at most two input sources
questions.answer: Uses bandwidth more efficiently when sources are bursty because unused slots are skipped, but requires per-slot addressing
questions.explanation: Statistical TDM dynamically allocates slots only to active sources, improving utilization for bursty traffic, but each slot must carry a source identifier. Under heavy load it can introduce queuing delay (not guaranteed lower latency), still uses framing, and is not limited to two sources.

questions.question: In CDMA, multiple users can share the same frequency band at the same time because:
options: Each user's data is multiplied by a distinct orthogonal (or near-orthogonal) spreading code
Each user is assigned a different time slot
Each user transmits on a different sub-band
The receiver uses an RTS/CTS handshake per user
questions.answer: Each user's data is multiplied by a distinct orthogonal (or near-orthogonal) spreading code
questions.explanation: CDMA gives each user a unique chip sequence; signals sum on air and receivers despread using their code, exploiting code orthogonality. Time slots describe TDMA, sub-bands describe FDMA, and RTS/CTS is a Wi-Fi MAC mechanism, not a multi-access technique.

questions.question: If a system uses 64-QAM with a symbol rate of 1 Msymbol/s, the raw bit rate is:
questions.answer: 6 Mbps
questions.explanation: log2(64) = 6 bits per symbol, so 1 Msymbol/s × 6 = 6 Mbps. 64 Mbps confuses constellation size with bits/symbol, 8 Mbps would be 256-QAM, and 1 Mbps would be BPSK.

questions.question: Which modulation scheme is typically more robust against amplitude noise: ASK, FSK, or PSK?
options: FSK and PSK are more robust than ASK because they encode information in frequency or phase, not amplitude
ASK is most robust because it uses only two levels
All three are equally affected by amplitude noise
PSK is the most affected because phase is fragile in copper media
questions.answer: FSK and PSK are more robust than ASK because they encode information in frequency or phase, not amplitude
questions.explanation: Amplitude noise directly corrupts ASK, whereas FSK and PSK tolerate amplitude disturbances because the carried information lives in frequency or phase. PSK is actually very power-efficient in noisy channels, and the three techniques have markedly different noise behavior, not equal.

questions.question: Chromatic dispersion in optical fiber refers to:
options: Different wavelengths of light travelling at slightly different speeds, broadening the pulse in time
Energy coupling between adjacent fibers in a cable
Random power fluctuations from the laser source
Reflection of light back toward the source at splices
questions.answer: Different wavelengths of light travelling at slightly different speeds, broadening the pulse in time
questions.explanation: Chromatic dispersion arises because the fiber's index of refraction varies with wavelength, so spectral components of a pulse arrive at different times. Fiber-to-fiber coupling is crosstalk, laser amplitude noise is RIN, and back-reflection is Fresnel reflection, not dispersion.

questions.question: Category 6a UTP is specified to support 10GBASE-T for channel lengths up to approximately:
questions.explanation: Cat 6a supports 10GBASE-T for the full 100 m structured cabling reach. Cat 6 only supports 10GBASE-T to ~55 m due to alien crosstalk. 10 m is the realm of SFP+ direct-attach copper, and 300 m exceeds copper Ethernet limits.

questions.question: A major practical drawback of plain ASK on twisted-pair lines is that it:
options: Is very sensitive to sudden amplitude changes from noise and attenuation
Cannot be demodulated by coherent receivers
Uses more bandwidth than FSK at the same bit rate
questions.answer: Is very sensitive to sudden amplitude changes from noise and attenuation
questions.explanation: ASK carries information entirely in amplitude, so impulse noise, fading, or attenuation easily flip decision regions. It can be demodulated coherently or non-coherently, typically occupies less bandwidth than FSK, and does not need a separate clock wire.

questions.question: In DWDM compared to CWDM, channel spacing is:
options: Much tighter (e.g., 100 GHz or 50 GHz) allowing many more channels per fiber
Wider (e.g., 20 nm) to tolerate uncooled lasers
The same but using different wavelengths
Irrelevant because DWDM uses a single wavelength
questions.answer: Much tighter (e.g., 100 GHz or 50 GHz) allowing many more channels per fiber
questions.explanation: DWDM typically uses 50/100 GHz grids in the C-band, packing 40–96+ channels per fiber with temperature-stabilized lasers. CWDM uses 20 nm spacing and ~8 channels with uncooled lasers. DWDM explicitly leverages many wavelengths, not one.

questions.question: Orthogonal Frequency-Division Multiplexing (OFDM) combats frequency-selective fading primarily by:
options: Dividing a wide band into many narrow orthogonal subcarriers, each seeing an approximately flat channel
Applying a single very high symbol-rate PSK across the band
Using CDMA spreading on each subcarrier
Using a guard frequency band around each user
questions.answer: Dividing a wide band into many narrow orthogonal subcarriers, each seeing an approximately flat channel
questions.explanation: OFDM turns a frequency-selective wideband channel into many narrowband flat subchannels, simplifying equalization and mitigating ISI. A single high-rate single-carrier PSK is the opposite approach. Subcarrier CDMA would be MC-CDMA (different scheme), and OFDM doesn't place FDM-style guards between users—it exploits subcarrier orthogonality.

questions.question: An SNR of 20 dB corresponds to a power ratio of approximately:
questions.explanation: SNR(dB) = 10·log10(ratio); 20 dB ⇒ ratio = 10^(20/10) = 100. 10 dB ≈ 10, 13 dB ≈ 20, and 23 dB ≈ 200. The 20-to-100 confusion is a classic dB pitfall.

questions.question: Differential Manchester encoding differs from standard Manchester in that:
options: The meaning of a bit is carried by whether or not a transition occurs at the start of the bit period
It removes the mid-bit transition entirely
It eliminates the need for any clock recovery
questions.answer: The meaning of a bit is carried by whether or not a transition occurs at the start of the bit period
questions.explanation: Differential Manchester retains the mid-bit transition for clocking but encodes the data as presence/absence of a start-of-bit transition, making it polarity-insensitive. Removing the mid-bit transition would destroy the clock recovery benefit, and three levels describe ternary codes such as MLT-3.

questions.question: How many bits per symbol does 8-PSK transmit?
questions.explanation: M-PSK carries log2(M) bits per symbol, so 8-PSK transmits log2(8)=3 bits/symbol. 2 is QPSK, 4 is 16-QAM or 16-PSK, and 8 is the constellation size, not the per-symbol bit count.

questions.question: Thermal noise power over a bandwidth B at absolute temperature T is given by:
questions.answer: N = k · T · B
questions.explanation: Johnson–Nyquist noise power is N = kTB, where k is Boltzmann's constant, T is temperature in Kelvin, and B is bandwidth in Hz. The other forms invert or square variables incorrectly.

questions.question: Near-end crosstalk (NEXT) in twisted-pair cabling is best mitigated by:
options: Twisting each pair with a unique lay length and adding shielding where needed
Increasing the transmit power on each pair
Removing termination resistors at both ends
Using half-duplex operation at all times
questions.answer: Twisting each pair with a unique lay length and adding shielding where needed
questions.explanation: Different twist rates cause adjacent pairs' coupled fields to average out, reducing capacitive/inductive coupling; shielding (F/UTP, S/FTP) further reduces NEXT. Increasing TX power makes coupling worse. Removing termination causes reflections. Half-duplex neither prevents crosstalk nor addresses its physical cause.

questions.question: A modulation scheme occupies roughly the same bandwidth for different constellation sizes, so increasing constellation size mainly:
options: Raises bit rate at the cost of requiring higher SNR to maintain the same bit-error rate
Decreases required SNR because symbols carry more information
Increases baud rate even when bit rate is unchanged
Eliminates the need for error correction coding
questions.answer: Raises bit rate at the cost of requiring higher SNR to maintain the same bit-error rate
questions.explanation: Denser constellations pack more bits per symbol but cram decision regions closer together, so the SNR needed for a target BER grows. Baud rate is independent of constellation size, error correction remains valuable (and often more necessary), and SNR requirements go up, not down.

questions.question: Byte stuffing is used in character-oriented framing to:
options: Insert an escape byte before any payload byte that collides with the flag byte
Insert a zero bit after five consecutive ones
Pad a frame to a fixed multiple of 8 bytes
Detect single-bit errors in the frame
questions.answer: Insert an escape byte before any payload byte that collides with the flag byte
questions.explanation: Byte stuffing preserves framing by preceding accidental flag-byte values in the payload with an ESC byte and unescaping on receive. Inserting a zero after five ones describes bit stuffing in HDLC. Padding and error detection are separate mechanisms (framing vs. CRC/parity).

questions.question: A single parity bit appended to a data word can:
options: Detect any odd number of bit errors but cannot correct any error
Correct any single-bit error but not detect double errors
Both detect and correct any single-bit error
Detect only burst errors of length 2
questions.answer: Detect any odd number of bit errors but cannot correct any error
questions.explanation: One parity bit flips only when an odd number of bits are in error, so it detects odd-weight error patterns and misses even-weight patterns. It provides no location information, so no correction. Hamming codes or two-dimensional parity are needed for single-error correction.

questions.question: Baseband transmission refers to:
options: Transmitting the original digital signal directly on the medium without modulation onto a carrier
Modulating the signal onto a very high RF carrier
Multiplexing many signals on frequency sub-bands
A technique exclusive to fiber-optic systems
questions.answer: Transmitting the original digital signal directly on the medium without modulation onto a carrier
questions.explanation: Baseband sends the signal at its native spectrum (e.g., Ethernet's 10BASE-T name: BASE = baseband). Broadband instead modulates onto carriers and often multiplexes bands. Baseband is common on both copper and fiber, not fiber-only.

questions.question: With 1 start bit, 8 data bits, and 1 stop bit per character in asynchronous transmission, the framing overhead per byte is:
questions.explanation: Total bits/char = 10; overhead bits = 2 (start+stop); 2/10 = 20%. 10% would be only one overhead bit; 12.5% is 1/8 and ignores that both start and stop are added; 25% would correspond to 2 overhead bits per 6 data bits.

questions.question: A received signal power of 1 mW is attenuated to 0.01 mW along a link. The attenuation in dB is:
questions.answer: 20 dB
questions.explanation: Attenuation(dB) = 10·log10(Pin/Pout) = 10·log10(100) = 20 dB. 10 dB would be a factor of 10, 2 dB ≈ a factor of 1.58, and 100 dB is a factor of 10^10.

questions.question: A channel has bandwidth 3 kHz and a linear SNR of 7 (not in dB). Its Shannon capacity is closest to:
questions.answer: 9000 bps
questions.explanation: C = B·log2(1+SNR) = 3000·log2(8) = 3000·3 = 9000 bps. 21000 bps would imply log2(1+SNR)=7, i.e., SNR=127. 3000 matches bandwidth only, and 30000 corresponds to ~10 bits/symbol.

questions.question: Why does Shannon's capacity formula generally give a lower value than Nyquist's on the same bandwidth?
options: Shannon accounts for noise and bounds the error-free rate regardless of how many levels you use, while Nyquist assumes a noiseless channel
Nyquist counts bits in both directions; Shannon counts only one
Shannon uses a logarithm in base e while Nyquist uses base 2
They are identical; the difference is only historical
questions.answer: Shannon accounts for noise and bounds the error-free rate regardless of how many levels you use, while Nyquist assumes a noiseless channel
questions.explanation: Shannon's bound includes thermal noise and caps reliable capacity even as L→∞, whereas Nyquist (noiseless) grows without bound with more levels. The duplexity, logarithm base, and historical-equivalence explanations are all incorrect.

questions.question: In OFDM, the subcarriers are described as orthogonal because:
options: Over one symbol period, the integral of the product of any two distinct subcarrier waveforms is zero
They are placed exactly 90° out of phase with each other
They are on different amplitudes to avoid interference
They are transmitted by different antennas
questions.answer: Over one symbol period, the integral of the product of any two distinct subcarrier waveforms is zero
questions.explanation: OFDM chooses subcarrier spacing Δf = 1/T where T is the symbol period, ensuring pairwise inner products integrate to zero over T and allowing a DFT to separate them. Phase relationships and amplitude scaling do not create orthogonality, and MIMO (different antennas) is a separate axis.

questions.question: Manchester encoding has a minimum required bandwidth roughly twice that of NRZ because:
options: Its guaranteed mid-bit transition doubles the fundamental signaling frequency
It transmits each bit twice for redundancy
It requires a separate clock channel on a second frequency
It uses a longer CRC polynomial
questions.answer: Its guaranteed mid-bit transition doubles the fundamental signaling frequency
questions.explanation: Manchester forces a transition every bit, creating a signaling element of half-bit duration, which pushes the fundamental frequency to roughly 1/Tb (versus 1/(2Tb) for NRZ). It doesn't send bits twice, need a separate clock channel, or involve CRC.

questions.question: Intersymbol interference (ISI) on a bandlimited channel is primarily caused by:
options: Pulse spreading in time so that energy from one symbol bleeds into adjacent symbol intervals
Two users transmitting on the same frequency band simultaneously
Thermal noise in the receiver amplifier
Incorrect line coding that removes DC balance
questions.answer: Pulse spreading in time so that energy from one symbol bleeds into adjacent symbol intervals
questions.explanation: Bandlimited channels distort pulses so tails spread, corrupting neighboring symbols—this is ISI and is why Nyquist pulse shaping (raised cosine) or equalization is used. Co-channel interference is CCI, thermal noise is AWGN, and DC imbalance causes baseline wander, not ISI per se.

questions.question: A well-known drawback of OFDM systems is high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) because:
options: Many subcarriers can add in phase, producing occasional large peaks that stress the power amplifier
Each subcarrier carries a different modulation scheme
Subcarriers overlap in frequency, summing in the frequency domain
OFDM demands constant-envelope modulation that cannot be back-off limited
questions.answer: Many subcarriers can add in phase, producing occasional large peaks that stress the power amplifier
questions.explanation: The time-domain OFDM signal is a sum of many subcarriers; when they align in phase, the instantaneous amplitude spikes, forcing amplifier back-off. The modulation mix, spectral overlap, and a constant-envelope assumption are not the reason—OFDM is explicitly non-constant envelope.

questions.question: In OFDM, making the subcarrier spacing Δf smaller (and hence symbol period T longer) has which pair of trade-offs?
options: Better spectral efficiency and longer guard interval tolerance, but more sensitivity to Doppler/frequency offset
Better Doppler tolerance but worse spectral efficiency
No effect on Doppler sensitivity, only on throughput
questions.answer: Better spectral efficiency and longer guard interval tolerance, but more sensitivity to Doppler/frequency offset
questions.explanation: Smaller Δf means narrower subcarriers—good for combating delay spread (longer T accommodates bigger CP) and packing more subcarriers, but any carrier-frequency offset or Doppler approaches a larger fraction of Δf, breaking orthogonality. The CP is still required regardless of Δf.

questions.question: BPSK requires a bit-energy-to-noise-density ratio Eb/N0 of approximately 9.6 dB for a BER of 10^-5 in AWGN. Which change would most reduce the required Eb/N0?
options: Adding a strong forward-error-correction (FEC) code such as LDPC
Doubling the constellation size to QPSK
Decreasing the receiver bandwidth below the symbol rate
questions.answer: Adding a strong forward-error-correction (FEC) code such as LDPC
questions.explanation: Modern FEC (LDPC, turbo) gets close to the Shannon limit and cuts required Eb/N0 by several dB. Doubling the constellation (QPSK at same bandwidth) keeps the same per-bit energy requirement in AWGN, sub-symbol-rate filtering causes ISI, and ASK is worse than PSK in AWGN.

questions.question: The 8B/10B line code used in Gigabit Ethernet and Fibre Channel provides DC balance primarily by:
options: Choosing between two complementary code words (running disparity) so transmitted 1s and 0s stay balanced over time
Scrambling the data with a long LFSR
Adding a parity bit every 8 bits
questions.answer: Choosing between two complementary code words (running disparity) so transmitted 1s and 0s stay balanced over time
questions.explanation: 8B/10B tracks running disparity and picks one of two encodings per 8-bit value so the long-term bit balance is near 50/50, preventing baseline wander. Alternating bit inversion doesn't guarantee balance, scrambling is a different technique (used in 64B/66B), and a parity bit addresses error detection, not DC balance.

questions.question: The group velocity of light in a fiber with effective refractive index 1.5 is about 2 × 10^8 m/s. One-way propagation delay over a 3000 km fiber link is therefore closest to:
questions.answer: 15 ms
questions.explanation: delay = distance / velocity = 3,000,000 m / 2×10^8 m/s = 0.015 s = 15 ms. 10 ms would require c in vacuum for this distance, 1.5 ms is 10× too small, and 30 ms would be a round-trip estimate.

questions.question: How many layers does the OSI reference model define?
questions.explanation: OSI defines 7 layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application. TCP/IP is often taught with 4 or 5 layers, not OSI. 8 is not a standard model.

questions.question: A router primarily operates at which OSI layer?
questions.answer: Network (Layer 3)
questions.explanation: Routers forward packets using Layer 3 addresses (IP). Layer 2 devices (switches/bridges) forward frames by MAC. Transport-layer devices are end hosts or load balancers. Layer 1 devices (hubs/repeaters) regenerate signals.

questions.question: An Ethernet switch primarily operates at which OSI layer?
questions.answer: Data Link (Layer 2)
questions.explanation: Classic switches forward frames using MAC addresses, the job of Layer 2. Routers are Layer 3, hubs are Layer 1, and firewalls/proxies may reach Layer 7. Multilayer (L3) switches add routing but are distinct from classic L2 switches.

questions.question: Which term best describes a network confined to a single building or campus under one administrative domain?
questions.answer: LAN
questions.explanation: A LAN (Local Area Network) spans a limited area like a home, office, or campus. WANs span cities or countries, MANs span a metro area, and PANs are personal-scale (Bluetooth, USB).

questions.question: In a star topology, every device connects:
options: To a central hub or switch
questions.answer: To a central hub or switch
questions.explanation: Star topology uses a central node so each host has its own link. Full connection between every pair is full mesh. A closed loop is ring. A shared backbone is bus.

questions.question: The Transport layer in the TCP/IP model is responsible for:
options: End-to-end communication between processes, including reliability (TCP) or best-effort (UDP) semantics
Physical signal encoding on the wire
Rendering content for display in a browser
questions.answer: End-to-end communication between processes, including reliability (TCP) or best-effort (UDP) semantics
questions.explanation: Transport multiplexes by port and optionally provides reliability, ordering, and flow/congestion control (TCP). Signal encoding is Physical, forwarding is Network, and rendering is entirely in the application domain.

questions.question: Which of the following is a classic example of a circuit-switched network?
options: The traditional public switched telephone network (PSTN)
questions.answer: The traditional public switched telephone network (PSTN)
questions.explanation: PSTN established a dedicated circuit end-to-end for the call duration. The Internet, Wi-Fi, and Ethernet LANs are packet-switched, not circuit-switched.

questions.question: The Protocol Data Unit (PDU) at the Transport layer of the OSI model is called a:
questions.answer: Segment (TCP) or datagram (UDP)
questions.explanation: Transport PDUs are segments (TCP) or datagrams (UDP). Frames are Layer 2, packets are Layer 3, and bits are Layer 1.

questions.question: The Physical layer is responsible for:
options: Representing bits as electrical, optical, or radio signals on the medium
Logical addressing and routing across networks
Encryption and decryption of application data
questions.answer: Representing bits as electrical, optical, or radio signals on the medium
questions.explanation: Layer 1 deals with signaling, connectors, line rates, and media. Routing/logical addresses are Layer 3, reliable byte streams are Layer 4 (TCP), and encryption sits at Layer 6 or in application protocols (TLS).

questions.question: Which organization primarily publishes the RFCs that standardize Internet protocols?
questions.answer: IETF
questions.explanation: The IETF publishes RFCs for Internet protocols (TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS, etc.). IEEE standardizes LAN/PHY (802.3, 802.11). ITU-T handles telecom (E.164, G.992). ISO governs ISO/IEC specifications such as the OSI model itself.

questions.question: The TCP/IP reference model most commonly contains how many layers?
questions.explanation: Classic TCP/IP has 4 layers: Link, Internet, Transport, Application. Some textbooks add a separate Physical layer to get 5. 7 is OSI, and 3 omits a required layer.

questions.question: End-to-end latency is commonly measured in:
questions.answer: Milliseconds (ms)
questions.explanation: Latency is a time, usually ms for Internet paths. Bits/s is throughput, dB is a logarithmic power ratio, and packets/s is a rate, not a delay.

questions.question: Which statement best characterizes a pure peer-to-peer (P2P) network?
options: Every participant can act as both client and server
There is always a single dedicated server
Clients can never talk to each other directly
Routing is performed only by ISPs
questions.answer: Every participant can act as both client and server
questions.explanation: P2P peers share responsibilities and resources symmetrically (e.g., BitTorrent). A dedicated server defines client-server. Direct peer communication is precisely what P2P enables. ISP routing is independent of the application model.

questions.question: A firewall that inspects packets based only on addresses and ports without tracking connection state is called:
questions.answer: A stateless packet filter
questions.explanation: Stateless filters apply rules per packet with no memory of prior packets. Stateful firewalls track TCP/UDP flows. Application proxies terminate and reconstruct Layer 7 traffic. NGFWs add identity, DPI, and IPS features.

questions.question: A MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) typically spans:
options: A city-sized area, larger than a LAN but smaller than a WAN
questions.answer: A city-sized area, larger than a LAN but smaller than a WAN
questions.explanation: MANs cover a metro region. A single building is usually a LAN, a nationwide network is a WAN, and a single cubicle is at most a PAN.

questions.question: Which OSI layer is traditionally responsible for translating between application data representations (e.g., character sets, compression, encryption)?
questions.answer: Presentation (Layer 6)
questions.explanation: Presentation handles syntax translation, encoding, compression, and (historically) encryption. Application is the user-facing service. Session manages dialogs/checkpoints. Transport handles end-to-end delivery.

questions.question: Compared with circuit switching, packet switching:
options: Achieves better link utilization for bursty traffic by statistically multiplexing many flows
Requires a call-setup phase for every conversation
Always uses dedicated bandwidth per conversation
questions.answer: Achieves better link utilization for bursty traffic by statistically multiplexing many flows
questions.explanation: Packet switching shares links and fills idle time with other flows, excellent for bursty data. Fixed delay and dedicated bandwidth are circuit-switching traits, and packet switching (at least datagram) needs no setup.

questions.question: A signal propagates through a 2400 km fiber at about 2 × 10^8 m/s. The one-way propagation delay is closest to:
questions.answer: 12 ms
questions.explanation: delay = 2,400,000 / 2×10^8 = 0.012 s = 12 ms. 8 ms would require c in vacuum, 1.2 ms is off by a factor of 10, and 24 ms would be round-trip.

questions.question: A 1500-byte packet is transmitted on a 100 Mbps link. The transmission (serialization) delay is:
questions.answer: 120 microseconds
questions.explanation: Transmission delay = frame bits / link rate = 12,000 / 10^8 = 1.2 × 10^-4 s = 120 μs. 1.2 ms would be a 10 Mbps link, 15 μs is incorrect bit counting, and 12 ms is off by 100×.

questions.question: For a link with bandwidth B and round-trip time RTT, the bandwidth-delay product (BDP) represents:
options: The amount of data that can be 'in flight' on the path at any instant
The maximum packet size the link can carry
The minimum window size for half-duplex operation
The total buffering available in all routers
questions.answer: The amount of data that can be 'in flight' on the path at any instant
questions.explanation: BDP = bandwidth × RTT is the pipe capacity in bits; TCP's congestion window needs to be at least this large to fill the pipe. It is unrelated to MTU, half-duplex rules, or router buffer pool size.

questions.question: The main drawback of a star topology is that:
options: Failure of the central hub or switch disconnects all attached nodes
Any single cable break isolates every node
Traffic must traverse all nodes in sequence
It cannot be extended beyond 10 hosts
questions.answer: Failure of the central hub or switch disconnects all attached nodes
questions.explanation: The central device is a single point of failure; a cut cable affects only the one host attached to it. Traversing every node is ring behavior. Star scales to thousands of ports in practice.

questions.question: A fully connected (full mesh) network with N nodes requires how many point-to-point links?
questions.explanation: Each pair of nodes needs one link; the number of pairs is N choose 2 = N(N-1)/2. N links is a ring or bus, 2N is double counted, and N^2 counts every ordered pair including self-loops.

questions.question: Compared with throughput, goodput explicitly excludes:
options: Retransmitted bits and protocol overhead, counting only useful application-layer data delivered
Bits sent on the forward path
questions.answer: Retransmitted bits and protocol overhead, counting only useful application-layer data delivered
questions.explanation: Goodput measures useful payload received at the app, subtracting headers and retransmits. Throughput counts all delivered bits including overhead and retransmits. Idle periods carry no bits, and parallel flows affect aggregate throughput, not this definition.

questions.question: A tier-1 ISP is distinguished by the fact that it:
options: Can reach every other network on the Internet purely through settlement-free peering, without paying transit
questions.answer: Can reach every other network on the Internet purely through settlement-free peering, without paying transit
questions.explanation: Tier-1 networks peer with all other tier-1s for free and thus do not purchase transit. Residential access is consumer ISP work, DNS roots are operated by 12 root server operators, and no single ISP owns all IXPs.

questions.question: An Internet Exchange Point (IXP) primarily exists to:
options: Allow multiple independent networks to peer with each other at a common location, often reducing cost and latency
Enforce BGP policy on behalf of ICANN
questions.answer: Allow multiple independent networks to peer with each other at a common location, often reducing cost and latency
questions.explanation: IXPs are Layer 2 fabrics where ISPs peer directly, keeping local traffic local. DNS root is separate, IP allocations come from RIRs (ARIN, RIPE, APNIC, LACNIC, AFRINIC), and BGP policy is set by each AS, not ICANN.

questions.question: At which OSI layer would you expect to find the function of segmentation and reassembly of an application byte stream?
questions.answer: Transport (Layer 4)
questions.explanation: TCP segments application streams into segments bounded by MSS and reassembles them on receive. L2 fragments rarely, L3 may fragment but not by design for stream delivery, and L5 manages dialog, not byte-stream boundaries.

questions.question: A virtual-circuit packet-switched network differs from a pure datagram network because it:
options: Uses a setup phase to install forwarding state along the path, so every packet follows the same route in order
Does not require addresses in the packet
questions.answer: Uses a setup phase to install forwarding state along the path, so every packet follows the same route in order
questions.explanation: Virtual circuits (X.25, ATM, MPLS LSPs) establish per-flow state so all packets take one route; datagram networks forward each packet independently. The wire is still shared, the VC label replaces (but doesn't eliminate) addressing, and VCs are connection-oriented.

questions.question: Which type of switching had to read, store, and then forward an entire variable-length message before any bit left the node, introducing high per-hop delay?
questions.answer: Message switching
questions.explanation: Message switching (early telegraph/store-and-forward systems) required whole-message buffering per hop. Circuit switching forwards in real time once the circuit is set up. Packet switching bounds hop delay by slicing into packets. Cut-through explicitly starts forwarding after reading only the header.

questions.question: On a store-and-forward packet switch with link rate R and packet size L, the per-hop delay for a single packet across N links (ignoring queuing and propagation) is:
questions.answer: N · (L/R)
questions.explanation: Each of the N hops must fully receive the packet before forwarding, so total transmission delay is N·L/R. L/R is one hop, L/(N·R) would mean hops help linearly (they don't), and dividing by R² is dimensionally wrong.

questions.question: A pure client-server architecture tends to hit scalability limits because:
options: The server's uplink bandwidth and CPU become a bottleneck as the number of clients grows
DNS can never resolve more than a fixed number of names per day
Routers drop any connection that lasts longer than 60 seconds
questions.answer: The server's uplink bandwidth and CPU become a bottleneck as the number of clients grows
questions.explanation: All load converges on the central server, so server capacity caps the system. TCP and DNS impose no such arbitrary caps, and there is no universal 60-second router timeout for sessions.

questions.question: A common trade-off of P2P file-distribution systems is that they:
options: Gain capacity as more peers join but may suffer from churn, NAT traversal, and uneven peer resources
Require a static public IP on every peer
Centralize all data in a single super-peer
questions.answer: Gain capacity as more peers join but may suffer from churn, NAT traversal, and uneven peer resources
questions.explanation: P2P systems self-scale with users but contend with peers joining/leaving, firewalls/NATs, and heterogeneous upload capacity. Static public IPs are not required (NAT hole-punching exists), super-peers are an architectural option not mandatory, and UDP signaling is widely used (STUN, ICE).

questions.question: An SLA promising 99.9% monthly availability allows a maximum monthly downtime of approximately:
questions.answer: 43 minutes
questions.explanation: One month ≈ 43,200 minutes; 0.1% of that ≈ 43 minutes. 4 minutes is the budget for 99.99%, 7 hours is closer to 99% (≈7.2 h), and 8 hours matches ~98.9%.

questions.question: Ethernet PHY and MAC specifications (for example, 802.3 and 802.11) are developed by which body?
questions.answer: IEEE
questions.explanation: IEEE 802 working groups publish LAN/MAN standards including Ethernet (802.3) and Wi-Fi (802.11). IETF owns the Internet protocol suite, ITU-T owns telecom standards, and W3C covers web technologies (HTML, CSS).

questions.question: The OSI Data Link Layer PDU is called a:
questions.answer: Frame
questions.explanation: L2 PDUs are frames (e.g., Ethernet frame). Packets are L3, segments L4, and bits L1.

questions.question: A gateway, in networking terminology, most commonly refers to:
options: A device that translates between different protocols or networks (often at Layer 3 or higher)
A synonym for a Layer 2 switch
Only a device that runs the DNS protocol
questions.answer: A device that translates between different protocols or networks (often at Layer 3 or higher)
questions.explanation: Gateways bridge protocol or architectural differences (e.g., SIP gateway between IP and PSTN, or a default gateway router). Splitters are passive RF devices, switches are L2 by default, and DNS servers are name-resolution hosts, not gateways in the general sense.

questions.question: A traditional Layer 2 Ethernet segment is contained within one:
questions.answer: Broadcast domain
questions.explanation: All hosts on an L2 segment receive each other's broadcasts; the broadcast domain ends at a router (or separated VLANs). A P2P link is a smaller case, an AS is a BGP concept, and each VLAN has its own broadcast domain/subnet.

questions.question: Which OSI layer establishes, manages, and terminates dialogs between applications, including checkpointing?
questions.answer: Session (Layer 5)
questions.explanation: Session handles dialog control, token management, and synchronization points. Transport is end-to-end delivery, Presentation handles data-format translation, and Application is user-facing services.

questions.question: A satellite link to a geostationary satellite has a one-way propagation delay of approximately:
questions.answer: About 120 ms
questions.explanation: GEO altitude is ~35,786 km; at c≈3×10^8 m/s one-way delay is about 120 ms (~240 ms RTT). LEO links are ~5–20 ms one-way. 1 ms is terrestrial short links, and 500 ms is close to RTT not one-way.

questions.question: End-to-end delay along a path is classically modeled as the sum of which four components per hop?
questions.answer: Processing, queuing, transmission, propagation
questions.explanation: Per-hop delay = proc + queue + trans + prop (Kurose/Ross). The other lists mix performance artifacts or application-layer steps and do not form the canonical per-hop delay budget.

questions.question: Within an enterprise network, VLANs are commonly used to:
options: Separate broadcast domains on a shared switch infrastructure without dedicated switches per group
Increase the link speed beyond 1 Gbps
Replace routers entirely at the edge
questions.answer: Separate broadcast domains on a shared switch infrastructure without dedicated switches per group
questions.explanation: VLANs partition L2 domains logically, allowing one switch fabric to host many segments. They do not raise line rate, cannot route on their own, and provide segmentation—not cryptographic—isolation; confidentiality needs encryption.

questions.question: On a link approaching capacity, average queuing delay according to M/M/1 intuition behaves as:
options: It rises nonlinearly and tends toward infinity as utilization ρ approaches 1
It stays constant regardless of utilization
It depends only on the propagation delay of the link
questions.answer: It rises nonlinearly and tends toward infinity as utilization ρ approaches 1
questions.explanation: Classical queuing theory (M/M/1) gives mean waiting time ∝ ρ/(1-ρ), blowing up as ρ→1. Queue delay rises with ρ, not decreases, and is unrelated to propagation delay (which is independent of offered load).

questions.question: On a 1 Gbps transcontinental link with an RTT of 100 ms, the bandwidth-delay product is closest to:
questions.explanation: BDP = 10^9 b/s × 0.1 s = 10^8 bits = 12.5 MB. A TCP window smaller than this underutilizes the pipe (classic reason to enable RFC 1323 window scaling). 100 MB overstates by ~8×, 1.25 MB is 10× too small, and 1 GB is 80× too large.

questions.question: Which of the following is best described as an overlay network?
options: A Tor or BitTorrent network that builds its own topology on top of the existing IP infrastructure
A physical fiber backbone between two buildings
A set of DNS caches inside one ISP
questions.answer: A Tor or BitTorrent network that builds its own topology on top of the existing IP infrastructure
questions.explanation: Overlays such as Tor, BitTorrent, and P2P DHTs create logical topologies whose links are tunnels over IP. Fiber, VLANs, and DNS caches are underlay components or services, not overlay networks in the architectural sense.

questions.question: A key advantage of datagram packet switching over virtual-circuit switching is:
options: No per-flow setup state in the core, which simplifies failover around failed links
Guaranteed in-order delivery with zero duplicate packets
Smaller per-packet headers because addresses are not carried
Constant end-to-end jitter independent of load
questions.answer: No per-flow setup state in the core, which simplifies failover around failed links
questions.explanation: Datagram routers reroute each packet independently if a link fails, with no VC teardown/reestablishment. In-order, duplicate-free delivery is not guaranteed by datagram networks, addresses are still in headers, and jitter varies with load.

questions.question: Which of the following violates the end-to-end argument as formulated by Saltzer, Reed, and Clark?
options: Implementing end-to-end reliability entirely in the network core while endpoints do nothing
Doing CRC checks on a single link to drop obviously corrupt frames early
Having applications choose between TCP and UDP
questions.answer: Implementing end-to-end reliability entirely in the network core while endpoints do nothing
questions.explanation: The end-to-end argument favors placing functions that require end-to-end semantics at the endpoints; putting all reliability in the core is the canonical counterexample. Endpoint TLS, per-link error detection as a performance hint, and TCP/UDP choice are consistent with the principle.

questions.question: Little's law states that in a stable system, the average number of items in the system equals:
options: Arrival rate × average time spent in the system
questions.answer: Arrival rate × average time spent in the system
questions.explanation: Little's law: L = λ · W, linking queue length, arrival rate, and time spent. The other expressions mix unrelated quantities and do not correspond to Little's result.

questions.question: In an enterprise WAN service (e.g., MPLS L3VPN), the Committed Information Rate (CIR) represents:
options: The minimum data rate the provider guarantees under normal network conditions
The theoretical maximum burst achievable only at 3 a.m.
The rate at which packets must be fragmented
The encryption throughput of the edge device
questions.answer: The minimum data rate the provider guarantees under normal network conditions
questions.explanation: CIR is the contracted baseline; traffic above CIR up to Peak Information Rate (PIR) may be policed or marked. It is not about time-of-day bursts, fragmentation, or crypto throughput.

questions.question: A multilayer (Layer 3) Ethernet switch differs most clearly from a classical router because it:
options: Performs longest-prefix IP forwarding in hardware ASICs while retaining L2 switching inside VLANs
Can only bridge within a single VLAN
Does not have a management plane
questions.answer: Performs longest-prefix IP forwarding in hardware ASICs while retaining L2 switching inside VLANs
questions.explanation: L3 switches run IP routing protocols and forward via hardware at wire speed, blurring the line with routers but keeping L2 switching features. They usually support OSPF/BGP, route between VLANs, and run full management planes.

questions.question: In a hierarchical star (tree) topology, redundant uplinks between layers are commonly used to:
options: Provide failover and load-sharing while Spanning Tree or equivalent protocols prevent loops
questions.answer: Provide failover and load-sharing while Spanning Tree or equivalent protocols prevent loops
questions.explanation: Redundant L2 uplinks need STP/RSTP/MSTP or TRILL/SPB/EVPN to avoid loops; modern fabrics also use LACP for load-sharing. Redundancy doesn't change MAC space, simplex rules, or VLAN needs.

questions.question: The Data Link layer's Protocol Data Unit is called a:
questions.answer: Frame
questions.explanation: L2 PDUs are frames (Ethernet frame, PPP frame). Packets are L3, segments L4. 'Cell' is the fixed-length ATM PDU, technically L2 but specifically named, and not the general L2 PDU.

questions.question: A MAC address is:
options: A 48-bit hardware identifier assigned to a network interface
questions.answer: A 48-bit hardware identifier assigned to a network interface
questions.explanation: IEEE EUI-48 MAC addresses are 48 bits. IPv4 addresses are 32 bits (logical), TCP/UDP ports are 16 bits, and IPv6 addresses are 128 bits.

questions.question: Which field at the end of an Ethernet frame is used for error detection?
options: FCS (Frame Check Sequence) containing a CRC-32
questions.answer: FCS (Frame Check Sequence) containing a CRC-32
questions.explanation: Ethernet uses a 32-bit CRC in the FCS. Single parity detects only odd-weight errors, the Internet checksum is used at L3/L4 (IP/TCP/UDP), and Hamming codes are correction codes not used in Ethernet FCS.

questions.question: CSMA/CD is the classical access method for:
questions.answer: Shared-medium (half-duplex) Ethernet
questions.explanation: CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense, Multiple Access, Collision Detection) was used on shared coax and half-duplex repeater Ethernet. Modern full-duplex switched links have no collisions. Wi-Fi uses CSMA/CA. Token Ring uses token passing.

questions.question: The minimum frame size of classic 10/100 Mbps Ethernet is:
questions.answer: 64 bytes
questions.explanation: The minimum Ethernet frame is 64 bytes (including 14-byte header + 4-byte FCS), ensuring the slot time is long enough for collision detection. 46 bytes is the minimum payload (not frame), 128 is arbitrary, and 1518 is the maximum frame (without VLAN tag).

questions.question: Bit stuffing in HDLC is used to:
options: Prevent the flag pattern 01111110 from appearing inside the payload by inserting a 0 after five consecutive 1s
questions.answer: Prevent the flag pattern 01111110 from appearing inside the payload by inserting a 0 after five consecutive 1s
questions.explanation: HDLC uses 01111110 as its delimiter; inserting a 0 after five 1s in the payload guarantees the flag cannot appear accidentally. Padding for minimum length, compression, and CRCs are different mechanisms.

questions.question: In stop-and-wait ARQ, the sender transmits:
options: One frame and waits for its ACK before sending the next frame
A whole window of frames without any ACK
All frames and only receives a final summary ACK
questions.answer: One frame and waits for its ACK before sending the next frame
questions.explanation: Stop-and-wait has effective window size 1. Pipelined protocols (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat) send a window of unacknowledged frames. Ending with a summary ACK is not stop-and-wait, and there is no urgent-only mode.

questions.question: A hub operates at which OSI layer and what does it do with incoming bits?
options: Layer 1 — regenerates and broadcasts bits on all other ports
Layer 2 — learns MAC addresses and forwards selectively
Layer 3 — routes packets using IP addresses
questions.answer: Layer 1 — regenerates and broadcasts bits on all other ports
questions.explanation: Hubs are physical-layer repeaters: bits in on one port are repeated on every other port. MAC learning is a switch (L2) function, routing is L3, and port load balancing is a transport-layer concept.

questions.question: Which field in an 802.1Q VLAN tag identifies the VLAN?
questions.answer: VID (12 bits)
questions.explanation: The VLAN Identifier (VID) is 12 bits, allowing 4094 usable VLANs. TPID (0x8100) marks the tag. PCP encodes 802.1p priority. DEI is the Drop Eligible Indicator. Only VID names the VLAN.

questions.question: Spanning Tree Protocol (STP, 802.1D) was designed to:
options: Prevent loops in Layer 2 switched networks that have redundant paths
Route IP packets along the shortest path
questions.answer: Prevent loops in Layer 2 switched networks that have redundant paths
questions.explanation: STP blocks redundant L2 links to form a loop-free tree. IP routing is a distinct L3 function, encryption is MACsec/IPsec/TLS, and DHCP handles IP assignment.

questions.question: An Ethernet broadcast frame has a destination MAC address of:
questions.answer: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF
questions.explanation: The all-ones MAC address is the L2 broadcast. The all-zeros address is reserved and invalid. 01:00:5E:... is a multicast range for IPv4 multicast, and FE:80 is an IPv6 link-local prefix, not a MAC address format.

questions.question: The main purpose of a switch's MAC address table is to:
options: Map destination MAC addresses to outgoing ports so frames are forwarded only where needed
Resolve IP addresses to domain names
questions.answer: Map destination MAC addresses to outgoing ports so frames are forwarded only where needed
questions.explanation: Switches learn source MACs per port and look up destinations for forwarding, avoiding the flood-everywhere behavior of a hub. DNS resolves names, ARP resolves IP↔MAC (stored on hosts), and CRCs are computed on the fly, not from this table.

questions.question: The Point-to-Point Protocol (PPP) frame format is derived from which older protocol?
questions.answer: HDLC
questions.explanation: PPP uses an HDLC-like framing with 01111110 flags and bit stuffing. Token Ring, FDDI, and ARCNET are different LAN architectures with unrelated frame structures.

questions.question: Which device builds its forwarding decisions exclusively on MAC addresses within a single collision domain?
questions.answer: Bridge/switch
questions.explanation: Bridges/switches forward by MAC and split collision domains per port. Routers use IP. Hubs forward bits blindly at L1 without any address lookup. NAT gateways manipulate L3/L4 headers.

questions.question: A Layer 2 Ethernet 'trunk' port typically:
options: Carries frames for multiple VLANs, each tagged with an 802.1Q VLAN ID
Is limited to one VLAN and never tags frames
Connects only to end hosts, never other switches
questions.answer: Carries frames for multiple VLANs, each tagged with an 802.1Q VLAN ID
questions.explanation: Trunks carry many VLANs using 802.1Q tags and typically connect switches or hypervisors. Access ports carry a single untagged VLAN. Trunks routinely connect switches and run at modern line rates.

questions.question: A sender uses Go-Back-N ARQ with a 3-bit sequence number field. The maximum allowed window size is:
questions.explanation: For Go-Back-N with an n-bit sequence number, max window = 2^n − 1 = 7 (to prevent the receiver from confusing old and new frames). Selective Repeat allows up to 2^(n-1) = 4. 8 is the total sequence space, and 3 is the number of bits.

questions.question: A sender uses Selective Repeat ARQ with a 4-bit sequence number. The maximum send and receive windows are each:
questions.explanation: Selective Repeat requires that sender and receiver windows each be ≤ 2^(n-1), so with n=4 the max is 8. 15 is the GBN limit, 16 is the total sequence space, and 4 is just the number of bits.

questions.question: A stop-and-wait link has transmission time Tf = 1 ms and one-way propagation delay Tp = 10 ms (ACK time ignored). Its utilization is closest to:
questions.explanation: U = Tf / (Tf + 2·Tp) = 1/(1+20) = 1/21 ≈ 4.8%. 1/11 forgets the doubling of propagation. 50% ignores delay entirely, and 1/20 forgets to include Tf.

questions.question: With sliding window size N, transmission time Tf and one-way propagation Tp, full link utilization requires:
questions.explanation: Full utilization means the sender is still transmitting when the first ACK arrives: N·Tf ≥ Tf + 2·Tp → N ≥ 1 + 2·Tp/Tf. The other forms drop the 1 or invert the ratio; N=1 leaves the pipe empty for most of the RTT.

questions.question: CRC-based error detection with a k-bit CRC is guaranteed to detect:
options: All burst errors of length ≤ k and all odd-weight errors if the polynomial has (x+1) as a factor
Any pattern of errors with probability 1
All errors below an 8-bit burst size only
questions.answer: All burst errors of length ≤ k and all odd-weight errors if the polynomial has (x+1) as a factor
questions.explanation: A degree-k CRC catches all bursts up to length k and, when its generator has (x+1) as a factor, all odd-weight errors. It cannot guarantee detection of every conceivable error pattern (probability of miss ≈ 2^-k), nor is it limited to single-bit or ≤8-bit bursts.

questions.question: Ethernet II uses the 16-bit field following the source MAC as a:
options: Type/EtherType indicating the encapsulated protocol (e.g., 0x0800 for IPv4)
questions.answer: Type/EtherType indicating the encapsulated protocol (e.g., 0x0800 for IPv4)
questions.explanation: Ethernet II uses EtherType; values ≥ 0x0600 distinguish it from 802.3 length (≤ 0x05DC). VLAN tag would be 0x8100 (TPID) as the first 16 bits, then the length/type comes after. The CRC is the trailer (FCS).

questions.question: A collision on a shared Ethernet is resolved by:
options: Backing off for a random time chosen from an exponentially growing interval
Transmitting twice as fast until one side gives up
Broadcasting an emergency 'abort' packet to the Internet
questions.answer: Backing off for a random time chosen from an exponentially growing interval
questions.explanation: Truncated binary exponential backoff picks a random slot from [0, 2^k−1] slots after the k-th collision, with k capped at 10 (up to 16 retries total). Doubling transmit rate, emergency broadcasts, and fixed 1-second waits are not the Ethernet algorithm.

questions.question: The Ethernet slot time is chosen to be at least:
options: Twice the maximum end-to-end propagation delay of the shared medium
The inter-frame gap plus the preamble
questions.answer: Twice the maximum end-to-end propagation delay of the shared medium
questions.explanation: Slot time ≥ 2×Tprop guarantees that a sender will still be transmitting when the earliest possible collision signal could return, enabling reliable collision detection. The other options are either unrelated or would allow undetected collisions.

questions.question: In the Go-Back-N protocol, what does the receiver do when a frame arrives out of order?
options: Discards it and re-sends the ACK for the highest in-order frame received
Buffers it and sends a selective NAK
Forwards it immediately to the upper layer
Requests retransmission of every previous frame individually
questions.answer: Discards it and re-sends the ACK for the highest in-order frame received
questions.explanation: GBN receivers accept only in-order frames, discarding anything out of order and re-acking the last good one; the sender's timeout forces retransmission from the lost frame onward. Buffering out-of-order frames is Selective Repeat. Selective NAKs aren't GBN, and upper-layer delivery must remain in-order.

questions.question: Compared with 2-dimensional parity, Hamming codes can:
options: Correct any single-bit error and detect all double-bit errors (extended Hamming)
questions.answer: Correct any single-bit error and detect all double-bit errors (extended Hamming)
questions.explanation: Extended Hamming (SECDED) corrects 1-bit and detects 2-bit errors by combining Hamming bits with an overall parity. Plain detection, compression, and encryption are not its function.

questions.question: The Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP, 802.1w) improves over classical STP mainly because it:
options: Converges in sub-second time using port role negotiation instead of the 30–50 s timers of 802.1D
Uses a completely different loop-free algorithm based on link-state routing
Eliminates the need for a root bridge
questions.answer: Converges in sub-second time using port role negotiation instead of the 30–50 s timers of 802.1D
questions.explanation: RSTP replaces the listen/learn timers with explicit handshakes between neighbors, typically converging in well under a second. It still builds a tree (root bridge), does not encrypt BPDUs, and is not link-state based.

questions.question: LACP (Link Aggregation Control Protocol, 802.3ad) is used to:
options: Bundle multiple physical links into one logical L2 link for higher bandwidth and redundancy
Elect a root bridge for a VLAN
questions.answer: Bundle multiple physical links into one logical L2 link for higher bandwidth and redundancy
questions.explanation: LACP negotiates and maintains link aggregation groups (LAGs/port-channels). Root bridge election is STP/RSTP. Hot-swap detection is a lower-level signaling function, and MACsec (802.1AE) is a different standard for encryption.

questions.question: The I/G (Individual/Group) bit in a MAC address is:
options: The least significant bit of the first octet; 0 = unicast, 1 = multicast/broadcast
The most significant bit of the last octet
A bit used by 802.1Q for priority
questions.answer: The least significant bit of the first octet; 0 = unicast, 1 = multicast/broadcast
questions.explanation: The LSB of the first octet distinguishes individual from group addresses. The U/L (universal/local) bit is the next bit of the first octet. IPv4 vs IPv6 does not change this, and priority is a separate 802.1p PCP field in the 802.1Q tag.

questions.question: A 'cut-through' switch begins forwarding a frame as soon as it has:
options: Received the entire frame including FCS
questions.answer: Received the destination MAC address
questions.explanation: Cut-through forwarding starts after reading the destination MAC (first 6 bytes after the preamble), minimizing switching latency. Waiting for the whole frame is store-and-forward. Waiting for EtherType or 64 bytes describes fragment-free variants but not canonical cut-through.

questions.question: Which of the following actions moves an 802.1Q-tagged frame from one switch to another without touching the VLAN tag?
options: Forwarding it across a trunk port configured for that VLAN
Receiving it on an access port in a different VLAN
Sending it through a Layer 3 SVI
questions.answer: Forwarding it across a trunk port configured for that VLAN
questions.explanation: Trunk ports preserve the 802.1Q tag between switches (except the native/untagged VLAN). Access ports strip or add tags, L3 SVIs terminate the L2 domain, and sending to VLAN 1 untagged changes the tag state.

questions.question: On a full-duplex switched Ethernet link, CSMA/CD is:
options: Disabled because there is no shared medium and therefore no collisions
Still active to detect cable faults
questions.answer: Disabled because there is no shared medium and therefore no collisions
questions.explanation: Full-duplex point-to-point links have independent TX and RX pairs; collisions cannot occur, so CSMA/CD is not used. Cable fault detection uses Ethernet's own PHY auto-negotiation / link-integrity, and CSMA/CA is a wireless method.

questions.question: Which mechanism was classically used in 802.3 Ethernet to prevent a receiver from being overwhelmed by fast senders?
questions.answer: 802.3x PAUSE frames (MAC-level flow control)
questions.explanation: PAUSE frames (EtherType 0x8808) tell the peer to stop transmitting for a time. TCP and ICMP operate at higher layers. STP blocks links to prevent loops, not to throttle senders.

questions.question: Which scenario benefits most from Selective Repeat compared with Go-Back-N?
options: High bandwidth-delay product links with non-negligible loss, where re-sending the whole window wastes capacity
A link with only one frame ever in flight
questions.answer: High bandwidth-delay product links with non-negligible loss, where re-sending the whole window wastes capacity
questions.explanation: SR retransmits only the lost frame, saving enormous bandwidth on long fat pipes with loss. On clean or low-BDP links the savings over GBN are minimal, and stop-and-wait by definition is not pipelined.

questions.question: An Ethernet switch 'floods' a unicast frame when:
options: The destination MAC is unknown in its MAC address table
The frame has a VLAN tag
The frame is longer than 1000 bytes
questions.answer: The destination MAC is unknown in its MAC address table
questions.explanation: Unknown-unicast flooding sends the frame to all ports in the VLAN except the ingress one while learning happens. 00:00:00:00:00:00 is invalid, VLAN tagging and frame length alone do not trigger flooding.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why byte stuffing is used with Ethernet-style framing?
options: Actually, Ethernet uses a fixed-length preamble + SFD + length delimiters, not byte stuffing — byte stuffing is used by character-oriented protocols like classic PPP to escape flag bytes
Ethernet uses byte stuffing on every packet
Ethernet stuffs bytes only in jumbo frames
Ethernet stuffs the CRC between fragments
questions.answer: Actually, Ethernet uses a fixed-length preamble + SFD + length delimiters, not byte stuffing — byte stuffing is used by character-oriented protocols like classic PPP to escape flag bytes
questions.explanation: Ethernet delimits frames via the preamble/SFD and a known length or EtherType; no byte stuffing is needed. Byte stuffing is a framing method for character-oriented protocols (e.g., PPP async mode) where a flag byte must be escaped in the payload.

questions.question: The CRC is computed on which portion of the Ethernet frame?
options: Destination MAC, source MAC, EtherType/Length, and payload (excluding preamble/SFD and the FCS itself)
The entire frame including the preamble and SFD
questions.answer: Destination MAC, source MAC, EtherType/Length, and payload (excluding preamble/SFD and the FCS itself)
questions.explanation: FCS covers the frame from destination MAC through end of payload; preamble/SFD are for clock recovery and are not protected. Covering only payload or only MACs would miss key fields, and the FCS cannot cover itself.

questions.question: Which pair of protocols are commonly layered on top of PPP to authenticate dial-up or PPPoE subscribers?
questions.answer: PAP and CHAP
questions.explanation: PPP uses LCP to negotiate, then authenticates via PAP (plaintext) or CHAP (challenge-response). WEP/WPA are Wi-Fi link security. Kerberos/RADIUS are higher-level AAA services (RADIUS often backs PPP auth but is not layered on it as the authentication exchange itself). HTTP(S) is application-layer.

questions.question: Ethernet MAC address aging in a switch:
options: Removes learned entries after a period of inactivity so moved devices are relearned on their new port
Assigns sequential IPs to new devices
questions.answer: Removes learned entries after a period of inactivity so moved devices are relearned on their new port
questions.explanation: Aging (default ~300 s) keeps the table fresh when hosts move. Encryption, storm control, and DHCP are separate mechanisms unrelated to MAC aging.

questions.question: A byte-count framing scheme is considered fragile because:
options: If the count field is corrupted, the receiver loses alignment and may misinterpret all subsequent frames
It requires a CRC over only the header
It is limited to exactly 256-byte frames
questions.answer: If the count field is corrupted, the receiver loses alignment and may misinterpret all subsequent frames
questions.explanation: Byte-count framing has no resynchronization pattern, so a corrupted count cascades. It can carry binary data, CRC scope is orthogonal, and there is no inherent 256-byte limit in the generic scheme.

questions.question: A 1500-byte Ethernet frame (including headers, excluding preamble) is transmitted on a 1 Gbps link. Its transmission time is closest to:
questions.answer: 12 microseconds
questions.explanation: t = 1500·8 / 10^9 = 12,000 ns = 12 μs. 120 μs would be 100 Mbps, 1.2 μs would be 10 Gbps, and 1500 μs treats the frame as 1.5 Mb on a slower link.

questions.question: A Go-Back-N sender sends frames 0..7 and frame 2 is lost; ACK cumulative progress from the receiver after a timeout on 2 will require the sender to retransmit:
options: Only the next frame after the last ACK
questions.answer: Frames 2 through 7
questions.explanation: GBN retransmits the lost frame and everything sent after it because the receiver discarded out-of-order frames. Only frame 2 would be Selective Repeat behavior. All eight frames is a misread of the algorithm, and 'only the next frame' is stop-and-wait.

questions.question: Consider CRC-CCITT (generator x^16 + x^12 + x^5 + 1). A 16-bit CRC guarantees detection of all burst errors of length:
options: Up to and including 15 bits only
questions.answer: Up to and including 16 bits
questions.explanation: A degree-k CRC detects all bursts of length ≤ k. With a 16-bit generator, bursts up to 16 bits are detected with certainty. Longer bursts are detected probabilistically (≈1−2^-15 or similar). 15 and 32 misstate the bound, and 8 is unrelated.

questions.question: A sender has a 5-bit sequence number and uses Selective Repeat. Choosing a window size of 16 and receiver window 16 is:
options: Safe: both windows equal 2^(n−1) = 16, which satisfies the SR constraint
Unsafe: window size must equal the full sequence space
Unsafe: SR requires n ≥ 6 for any window
questions.answer: Safe: both windows equal 2^(n−1) = 16, which satisfies the SR constraint
questions.explanation: SR allows sender and receiver windows up to 2^(n-1); with n=5 that is 16. The equal-full-space option breaks the disambiguation property, and the 'window must be <' statement is stricter than necessary. There is no rule that n ≥ 6.

questions.question: Carrier extension in Gigabit Ethernet half-duplex mode was introduced because:
options: At 1 Gbps the unchanged 64-byte minimum frame was too short to allow reliable collision detection on the classic 200 m shared medium
Gigabit frames had to be longer for CRC coverage
Half-duplex was removed, so extension was decorative
questions.answer: At 1 Gbps the unchanged 64-byte minimum frame was too short to allow reliable collision detection on the classic 200 m shared medium
questions.explanation: GbE raised the slot time to 512 bytes for shared-medium operation by padding frames with carrier extension so CSMA/CD could still detect late collisions. CRC coverage, 'decorative' explanations, and encryption are not the reason.

questions.question: Tagged VLAN 1 frames crossing a native-VLAN-mismatched trunk typically result in:
options: A VLAN hopping / broadcast leakage vulnerability where frames can reach an unintended VLAN
A CRC failure at the first switch
questions.answer: A VLAN hopping / broadcast leakage vulnerability where frames can reach an unintended VLAN
questions.explanation: Mismatched native VLANs on a trunk let attackers craft double-tagged frames that strip one tag and reach another VLAN. Links usually don't shut down, CRCs still pass, and switches don't auto-reconfigure native VLANs.

questions.question: A network administrator sees MAC flapping events on a switch — the same MAC appears on two different ports repeatedly. The most likely root cause is:
options: A physical or logical loop in the L2 topology, often caused by a disabled/broken STP
An overfull ARP cache on the router
questions.answer: A physical or logical loop in the L2 topology, often caused by a disabled/broken STP
questions.explanation: Loops cause the same MAC to be seen via multiple paths, driving flap and broadcast storms. VLAN count, ARP pressure, and jumbo-frame issues produce different symptoms (broken connectivity, MTU drops) not MAC flapping.

questions.question: Which feature prevents a rogue host from claiming to be the STP root bridge by sending superior BPDUs on an edge port?
questions.answer: BPDU Guard
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard disables an edge (PortFast) port the moment a BPDU arrives, preventing topology takeover. PortFast alone only skips learning/listening; storm control rate-limits broadcasts; L3 ACLs don't affect BPDUs.

questions.question: MAC learning on a switch is a reactive process that combines:
options: Source-MAC learning on every received frame plus destination-MAC lookup at forwarding time
questions.answer: Source-MAC learning on every received frame plus destination-MAC lookup at forwarding time
questions.explanation: Switches learn the source MAC of each ingress frame against the ingress port and look up the destination MAC to decide egress. SNMP polling is management-plane. BPDUs don't carry end-host MACs. ARP snooping assists L3 features (e.g., DAI) but isn't the MAC-learning mechanism itself.

questions.question: Given a 2-D parity scheme with an M×N bit block and one row-parity bit per row plus one column-parity bit per column, this code can:
options: Correct any single-bit error (by using the row/column crossing) and detect many multi-bit patterns
Only detect errors, never correct them
Correct any double-bit error within the block
Correct any burst error of arbitrary length
questions.answer: Correct any single-bit error (by using the row/column crossing) and detect many multi-bit patterns
questions.explanation: A flipped bit causes exactly one failing row parity and one failing column parity, pinpointing the error for correction. Two simultaneous flips can mask each other in some patterns, so 2-D parity cannot correct all 2-bit or arbitrary bursts—but single-bit correction is solid.

questions.question: An IPv4 address is how many bits long?
questions.explanation: IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses, usually dotted-decimal (e.g., 192.0.2.1). MAC is 48 bits, IPv6 is 128 bits, and 64 bits matches the EUI-64 identifier used within IPv6 host portions.

questions.question: The default (natural) mask of the IPv4 class B range is:
questions.explanation: Classful rules: class A = /8, class B = /16, class C = /24. /32 is a host route. Classful addressing is largely replaced by CIDR in modern networks but shows up on exams.

questions.question: Which ICMP message type is used by the 'ping' tool to test reachability?
questions.answer: Echo Request (Type 8) and Echo Reply (Type 0)
questions.explanation: Ping sends ICMP Echo Request and expects Echo Reply. Destination Unreachable indicates delivery problems, Redirect is a gateway hint, and Time Exceeded is what 'traceroute' receives as TTLs expire.

questions.question: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) in IPv4 maps:
options: An IP address to its corresponding MAC address on the same subnet
A domain name to an IP address
An IP address to a port number
A MAC address to an autonomous system number
questions.answer: An IP address to its corresponding MAC address on the same subnet
questions.explanation: ARP resolves L3 IP to L2 MAC within a broadcast domain. DNS resolves names to IPs. Port numbers are at the transport layer. AS numbers are used in BGP, not ARP.

questions.question: An IPv6 address is written as:
options: Eight 16-bit hexadecimal groups separated by colons (e.g., 2001:db8::1)
Four decimal octets separated by dots (e.g., 192.0.2.1)
Six hexadecimal bytes separated by colons
