questions.answer: There is no dominant line-of-sight path and the received signal is the sum of many reflections
questions.explanation: Rayleigh applies in NLoS multipath; with a dominant LoS component, Rician fading is used. Single-path or noiseless assumptions aren't typical in real wireless channels.

questions.question: A free-space path loss (FSPL) at distance d and frequency f (in GHz) increases by how much when distance doubles?
questions.answer: ~6 dB
questions.explanation: FSPL scales as 1/d² → 20·log10(2) = 6.02 dB per doubling of distance. 3 dB is a halving of power/bandwidth, not doubling distance. 10 and 20 are off.

questions.question: In LTE, the physical resource block (PRB) is composed of:
options: 12 subcarriers × 1 slot (0.5 ms) in the frequency-time grid
questions.answer: 12 subcarriers × 1 slot (0.5 ms) in the frequency-time grid
questions.explanation: An LTE PRB is 12 subcarriers (180 kHz wide) × 7 or 6 symbols depending on CP, spanning 0.5 ms. The other choices are not standard PRB definitions.

questions.question: Wi-Fi roaming between APs within the same SSID is typically implemented at the Wi-Fi layer using:
options: Reassociation requests and optional 802.11r/k/v assistance for fast/steered roaming
A new DHCP lease each time
A new SSID advertisement each time
questions.answer: Reassociation requests and optional 802.11r/k/v assistance for fast/steered roaming
questions.explanation: Clients reassociate to the new AP, with 802.11r (FT) speeding 4-way handshake, 802.11k advising neighbor lists, and 802.11v helping steering. Captive portals and new SSIDs are not standard roaming steps.

questions.question: A Zigbee network with a coordinator, several routers, and end-devices forms which topology?
questions.answer: Mesh (tree-and-mesh capable)
questions.explanation: Zigbee typically forms meshes where routers relay messages and end-devices attach to routers. Pure bus and ring are not Zigbee topologies, and star is a special case the network can support.

questions.question: EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) is calculated as:
options: Transmit power (dBm) + antenna gain (dBi) − cable losses (dB)
questions.answer: Transmit power (dBm) + antenna gain (dBi) − cable losses (dB)
questions.explanation: EIRP measures the radiated power equivalent to an isotropic antenna; add TX and antenna gain, subtract feedline losses. RX-based or gain-only formulas ignore TX contributions.

questions.question: 5G 'sub-6 GHz' and 'mmWave' bands differ in that:
options: mmWave offers huge bandwidth and capacity but short range and sensitivity to blockage; sub-6 GHz offers coverage and penetration with more moderate capacity
sub-6 GHz has wider bandwidth than mmWave
questions.answer: mmWave offers huge bandwidth and capacity but short range and sensitivity to blockage; sub-6 GHz offers coverage and penetration with more moderate capacity
questions.explanation: mmWave (24–70 GHz) has wide channels but attenuates fast, while sub-6 GHz (e.g., 3.5 GHz) penetrates and covers broadly. The other statements are simply wrong.

questions.question: Wi-Fi channel bonding in 2.4 GHz often causes:
options: Severe adjacent-channel interference because of few non-overlapping channels in the band
questions.answer: Severe adjacent-channel interference because of few non-overlapping channels in the band
questions.explanation: The ISM 2.4 GHz band only has a few non-overlapping 20 MHz channels; 40 MHz bonds overlap with neighbors and degrade everyone. Throughput isn't guaranteed, and DFS is a 5 GHz feature.

questions.question: Calculate the approximate free-space path loss at 2.4 GHz and 100 m distance (FSPL(dB) = 20·log10(d) + 20·log10(f) + 32.44 with d in km and f in MHz).
questions.answer: About 80 dB

questions.question: The hidden terminal problem in a CSMA/CA network is NOT fully solved by RTS/CTS when:
options: Stations hear only the RTS or only the CTS due to asymmetric ranges, or when exposed terminals get unnecessarily blocked
Stations all hear each other perfectly
All stations share the same AP
questions.answer: Stations hear only the RTS or only the CTS due to asymmetric ranges, or when exposed terminals get unnecessarily blocked
questions.explanation: Asymmetric coverage means not every station hears both; RTS/CTS assumes symmetric hearing. Perfect hearing removes the problem altogether. Same AP and channel idleness are not root causes of the RTS/CTS failure modes.

questions.question: In 802.11, why does a station select a random backoff value rather than a constant?
options: To avoid synchronous retransmissions that would re-collide deterministically after a collision
To increase throughput guaranteed by the spec
questions.answer: To avoid synchronous retransmissions that would re-collide deterministically after a collision
questions.explanation: Random backoff desynchronizes competing stations. Throughput guarantees are statistical, FCC rules mandate spectrum behavior not backoff, and backoff is unrelated to encryption.

questions.question: 802.11 MU-MIMO requires the AP to know the channel state information (CSI) because:
options: Precoding across user streams depends on the per-client channel matrix to minimize cross-user interference
All users use the same antennas
questions.answer: Precoding across user streams depends on the per-client channel matrix to minimize cross-user interference
questions.explanation: MU-MIMO's zero-forcing (or similar) precoder inverts CSI to null interference between users. Shared antennas or fixed power do not eliminate the need for per-user channel info. CSI feedback is essential.

questions.question: LTE uses SC-FDMA on the uplink instead of OFDMA primarily because:
options: SC-FDMA has lower peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR), extending battery life and simplifying UE power amplifiers
It is spectrally more efficient than OFDMA
It eliminates the need for cyclic prefix
It supports more users per cell
questions.answer: SC-FDMA has lower peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR), extending battery life and simplifying UE power amplifiers
questions.explanation: Low PAPR is a big deal for battery-powered UE amplifiers. SC-FDMA is not more spectrally efficient than OFDMA, still uses CP, and its user-count advantage over OFDMA is modest. It's a classic UE-centric trade-off.

questions.question: A KRACK attack against WPA2 exploits:
options: Reinstallation of nonces in the 4-way handshake, allowing key-stream reuse
A flaw in 802.11r only (no effect on WPA2-Personal)
questions.answer: Reinstallation of nonces in the 4-way handshake, allowing key-stream reuse
questions.explanation: KRACK forces the client to reinstall an already-used key, resetting nonces and enabling plaintext recovery in CCMP. WPA3 SAE is the target of Dragonblood, not KRACK. RC4 belongs to WEP/TKIP. 802.11r was one path but not the only scope.

questions.question: A cellular 'soft handover' (classical CDMA) differs from hard handover by:
options: The UE is connected to two cells simultaneously during the transition, combining signals for diversity
The UE loses the old cell before acquiring the new one
The handover decision is always made by the core network
The UE changes IMSI during the handover
questions.answer: The UE is connected to two cells simultaneously during the transition, combining signals for diversity
questions.explanation: CDMA soft handover uses macro-diversity (make-before-break). Hard handover is break-before-make, classic in GSM/LTE. Handover decisions involve both RAN and core depending on type, and IMSI does not change at handover.

questions.question: With 5G 'network slicing', different tenants or services share the same radio/core while each slice:
options: Gets its own logical end-to-end virtual network with distinct SLAs, QoS, and security policies
Has its own physical cell tower
Is always delivered over mmWave only
Uses a unique SIM card per slice
questions.answer: Gets its own logical end-to-end virtual network with distinct SLAs, QoS, and security policies
questions.explanation: Slicing is a logical partitioning atop a shared 5G infrastructure, enabling tailored service guarantees per tenant. Dedicated physical infrastructure or SIMs per slice isn't required. Slices can span sub-6 and mmWave.

questions.question: Which cloud service model delivers virtual machines, storage, and networking as primitives that the customer manages?
questions.answer: IaaS
questions.explanation: IaaS (AWS EC2, Azure VMs) exposes compute, storage, and network primitives with the customer running the OS and apps. PaaS abstracts the OS/runtime. SaaS is a ready application. FaaS (Lambda) runs short-lived functions.

questions.question: AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, and Azure Functions are examples of which service model?
questions.answer: FaaS (Function-as-a-Service)
questions.explanation: FaaS runs small event-driven functions with no managed servers. IaaS gives VMs, SaaS delivers apps, and CaaS (Container-as-a-Service) offers container orchestration.

questions.question: Gmail, Microsoft 365, and Salesforce are classic examples of:
questions.answer: SaaS
questions.explanation: These are fully managed applications delivered to end users — canonical SaaS. IaaS/PaaS/FaaS are lower-level developer-facing models.

questions.question: A Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisor:
options: Runs directly on the hardware without a host OS
Runs as an application inside Windows or macOS
Is another name for a container runtime
Requires a Linux kernel to schedule VMs
questions.answer: Runs directly on the hardware without a host OS
questions.explanation: Type 1 (ESXi, Xen, KVM when viewed as kernel module + hardware) manages VMs directly on hardware. Type 2 (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) runs atop a host OS. Containers are different, and Linux kernel specifically is not a requirement.

questions.question: Which of the following is a NIST essential cloud characteristic?
questions.answer: On-demand self-service
questions.explanation: NIST SP 800-145 lists: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service. 'Always free', 'single-tenant by default', and 'physical co-location' are not characteristics.

questions.question: A Kubernetes pod is:
options: The smallest deployable unit, consisting of one or more containers sharing network and storage
questions.answer: The smallest deployable unit, consisting of one or more containers sharing network and storage
questions.explanation: Pods share a Linux network namespace and volumes among their containers. Servers are nodes, registries are image stores, and YAML is just configuration syntax.

questions.question: A cloud 'region' typically consists of:
options: Multiple availability zones with isolated power and networking within a geographic area
questions.answer: Multiple availability zones with isolated power and networking within a geographic area
questions.explanation: Regions group AZs for redundancy; AZs are physically separated but low-latency connected. Single DCs or PoPs do not give AZ-level isolation.

questions.question: The shared responsibility model generally holds that in IaaS:
options: The provider secures the physical infrastructure; the customer secures the OS, applications, data, and configuration
The customer manages the physical hardware
The provider manages the customer's OS patches
No one is responsible for security
questions.answer: The provider secures the physical infrastructure; the customer secures the OS, applications, data, and configuration
questions.explanation: IaaS customers retain a larger responsibility surface (OS up). In SaaS the provider handles most of the stack. Physical hardware is the provider's job; both parties share responsibility for configuration.

questions.question: An 'availability zone' typically provides:
options: Redundant isolation within a region — distinct power, cooling, and networking
questions.answer: Redundant isolation within a region — distinct power, cooling, and networking
questions.explanation: AZs are designed so one can fail without taking down another in the same region. Free tiers, cross-region replication, and CDNs are separate services or billing constructs.

questions.question: Object storage services like Amazon S3 are best used for:
options: Large, unstructured blobs accessed via HTTPS APIs, with high durability and flat namespaces
Hosting a block-level OS boot volume
As a direct mount for POSIX-dependent legacy apps
questions.answer: Large, unstructured blobs accessed via HTTPS APIs, with high durability and flat namespaces
questions.explanation: Object storage is API-accessed, scalable, and durable, ideal for blobs. Block storage (EBS) handles OS boot volumes. Relational queries need databases. POSIX mounts are file storage (EFS/NFS).

questions.question: Horizontal autoscaling adds capacity by:
options: Launching more instances or replicas in parallel
Moving to a larger single VM
questions.answer: Launching more instances or replicas in parallel
questions.explanation: Horizontal (scale-out) adds replicas; vertical (scale-up) grows a single instance. Storage attachment and clock speed changes aren't autoscaling.

questions.question: Docker containers share the host kernel and isolate processes using:
questions.answer: Linux namespaces and cgroups
questions.explanation: Namespaces isolate PID/net/mount/user and cgroups limit resources; both share one kernel. Full hypervisors belong to VMs, UML is historical, and recompiling the kernel is not per-container.

questions.question: A cloud Load Balancer operating at Layer 7 can route based on:
options: URL path, HTTP headers, cookies, and method
questions.answer: URL path, HTTP headers, cookies, and method
questions.explanation: L7 LBs (HAProxy, ALB, Envoy) understand HTTP semantics. MAC addresses or TTL are lower layers. AS-path is BGP routing.

questions.question: Which acronym describes a measurement of a specific aspect of service quality, like request latency or error rate?
questions.answer: SLI (Service Level Indicator)
questions.explanation: SLIs are measurable metrics; SLOs are targets for SLIs; SLAs are contractual commitments. 'SLP' isn't standard here.

questions.question: IAM (Identity and Access Management) in the cloud is used to:
options: Grant principals (users/roles) permissions to perform actions on resources
questions.answer: Grant principals (users/roles) permissions to perform actions on resources
questions.explanation: IAM centralizes authN/authZ and role assumption. DDoS detection, serverless execution, and object storage are distinct services.

questions.question: PaaS differs from IaaS because it:
options: Abstracts away the OS and runtime, letting developers focus on application code and config
Gives the developer root shell on every VM
Is only suitable for mobile apps
questions.answer: Abstracts away the OS and runtime, letting developers focus on application code and config
questions.explanation: PaaS (Heroku, App Engine, Elastic Beanstalk) hides OS and often runtime details behind buildpacks/runtimes. Root shells exist at IaaS level, storage-only isn't PaaS, and PaaS is not mobile-specific.

questions.question: Amazon S3's '11 nines' durability for standard storage means approximately:
options: 99.999999999% annual durability — losing 0.000000001% of objects per year on average
A 1-in-10 chance of loss per year
questions.answer: 99.999999999% annual durability — losing 0.000000001% of objects per year on average
questions.explanation: Durability is about data loss, not access; availability is separate and lower (e.g., 99.99% SLA). 1-in-10 loss is obviously wrong; 3- or 4-nines is far below 11 nines.

questions.question: A Kubernetes Deployment primarily provides:
options: Declarative management of ReplicaSets, rolling updates, and rollbacks for stateless workloads
questions.answer: Declarative management of ReplicaSets, rolling updates, and rollbacks for stateless workloads
questions.explanation: Deployments manage Pod replica lifecycle and rollouts. Volumes, multi-region clustering, and load balancing are other objects/features (PVC, federation/fleet, Services/Ingress).

questions.question: The CAP theorem states that during a network partition, a distributed system can provide at most:
options: Either consistency or availability, but not both, for every read/write
questions.answer: Either consistency or availability, but not both, for every read/write
questions.explanation: CAP: under partition (P), choose C or A per operation. It's a partition-time trade-off; when the network is healthy systems can be CA-like. Latency, throughput, and scalability aren't CAP claims.

questions.question: Eventual consistency means:
options: If no new updates are made, all replicas eventually converge to the same value
All replicas always show the same value at every instant
Reads always return the freshest value
questions.answer: If no new updates are made, all replicas eventually converge to the same value
questions.explanation: It permits temporary divergence but promises convergence given quiescence. Strict simultaneity is strong consistency. Rejecting writes or always returning freshest reads are different semantics.

questions.question: A 'cold start' in a serverless function refers to:
options: The latency incurred when an execution environment must be provisioned from scratch before the function runs
The first deployment of the function
questions.answer: The latency incurred when an execution environment must be provisioned from scratch before the function runs
questions.explanation: Cold starts include container image pull, runtime boot, and init code. Warm starts reuse a kept-alive environment. It isn't about deployment or cold storage.

questions.question: Which consistency model allows a client to read its own most recent writes but makes no guarantee across clients?
questions.answer: Read-your-writes consistency
questions.explanation: RYW guarantees monotonic views for the same session/client. Linearizability and serializability are strong; causal consistency orders operations by causal dependencies but is strictly stronger than RYW.

questions.question: Which load balancing algorithm distributes new connections to the server with the fewest active connections?
questions.answer: Least connections
questions.explanation: Least-connections balances long-lived or heterogeneous workloads better than strict RR. Round robin distributes evenly regardless of load. IP hash pins clients to servers. Random is stateless uniform.

questions.question: Which storage option is typically most suitable for a POSIX-compatible shared filesystem across VMs?
options: Network-attached file storage (e.g., NFS, EFS, Azure Files)
Object storage with no POSIX semantics
questions.answer: Network-attached file storage (e.g., NFS, EFS, Azure Files)
questions.explanation: File services expose POSIX-like semantics and can be mounted by many clients. Object storage is flat and eventually consistent, local NVMe isn't shared, and CDN buckets aren't full filesystems.

questions.question: A common reason to prefer private over public cloud is:
options: Regulatory, data residency, or performance requirements that mandate dedicated infrastructure
Cheaper short-term costs for small workloads
questions.answer: Regulatory, data residency, or performance requirements that mandate dedicated infrastructure
questions.explanation: Private clouds fit regulated industries (finance, health, gov) or workloads needing predictable infrastructure. They typically cost more short term and don't automatically provide CDN or global footprint.

questions.question: Which Kubernetes object exposes a set of Pods as a single network endpoint with stable virtual IP?
questions.answer: Service
questions.explanation: Services provide cluster-internal (ClusterIP) or external (NodePort/LoadBalancer) endpoints with selectors. Deployments manage rollouts, ConfigMaps carry configuration, and Jobs run finite batch workloads.

questions.question: In an Infrastructure as Code tool like Terraform, state files are critical because they:
options: Record which real-world resources correspond to declarations, enabling diff-based plans and updates
questions.answer: Record which real-world resources correspond to declarations, enabling diff-based plans and updates
questions.explanation: Terraform state maps config to actual IDs and remembers metadata; without it, plans would be unreliable. It may hold secrets (hence remote encrypted state), but that's not its essence. It doesn't encrypt the workspace or replace the console.

questions.question: A multi-region active-active deployment typically trades:
options: Higher availability and lower latency to users against the complexity of handling cross-region consistency and conflict resolution
Single point of failure for simplicity
questions.answer: Higher availability and lower latency to users against the complexity of handling cross-region consistency and conflict resolution
questions.explanation: Active-active across regions needs conflict handling (CRDTs, last-writer-wins, DBMS-level coordination). It doesn't give 'free' linearizability. Cost usually rises, and it eliminates single points of failure rather than adding them.

questions.question: Container image layering (e.g., in Docker) improves build/pull efficiency because:
options: Unchanged layers are reused across images and the daemon only transfers missing layers
It adds redundancy by duplicating layers
questions.answer: Unchanged layers are reused across images and the daemon only transfers missing layers
questions.explanation: CAS-based layering allows deduplication and incremental pulls. Encryption, hiding vulns (actually, scanning helps surface them), and duplication aren't layering's purpose.

questions.question: AWS S3 Glacier and similar 'archive tier' storage trades off:
options: Dramatically lower storage cost against higher retrieval latency (minutes to hours)
questions.answer: Dramatically lower storage cost against higher retrieval latency (minutes to hours)
questions.explanation: Archive tiers are cheap per GB but slow to read. Durability is typically similar, not lower. The trade-off is cost vs retrieval latency.

questions.question: A Blue-Green deployment strategy works by:
options: Running two production environments and switching traffic atomically from the old (blue) to the new (green)
Pushing every commit directly to prod
Running half the traffic on a canary and half on the new version
questions.answer: Running two production environments and switching traffic atomically from the old (blue) to the new (green)
questions.explanation: Blue-Green enables fast rollback by redirecting traffic. Canary splits traffic, direct-to-prod is trunk-based without staged cutover, and simultaneous reboots cause outages.

questions.question: FaaS platforms typically impose which constraint on function execution?
options: A bounded maximum runtime (e.g., 15 minutes on AWS Lambda) and memory per invocation
Infinite-duration background processes with no memory limit
Direct access to host kernel modules
Persistent writable local disk across invocations
questions.answer: A bounded maximum runtime (e.g., 15 minutes on AWS Lambda) and memory per invocation
questions.explanation: FaaS sandboxes are short-lived and resource-capped. Kernel module access and persistent writable disks aren't supported. Long daemons belong elsewhere (ECS, Fargate, VMs).

questions.question: Which design principle reduces blast radius in cloud architectures?
options: Partitioning workloads into cells/shards so failures in one cell don't impact others
Pooling all accounts into a single project
Mixing dev and prod in the same VPC
questions.answer: Partitioning workloads into cells/shards so failures in one cell don't impact others
questions.explanation: Cell-based / shuffle-shard architectures and multiple accounts/projects limit blast radius. Pooling everything or mixing environments does the opposite. Shared credentials undermine isolation.

questions.question: Which cloud networking primitive isolates resources and controls traffic at the subnet and security-group level?
questions.answer: VPC (AWS) / VNet (Azure) / VPC (GCP)
questions.explanation: VPC/VNet provide isolated virtual networks with subnets, route tables, NACLs, and security groups. Registries store images, runtimes run code, IAM controls identities/permissions.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary benefit of Immutable Infrastructure?
options: Replacing rather than mutating servers eliminates configuration drift and simplifies rollback
It reduces the number of needed VMs
It eliminates the need for backups
It forbids all manual changes via IAM
questions.answer: Replacing rather than mutating servers eliminates configuration drift and simplifies rollback
questions.explanation: Immutable infra builds golden images and replaces on change. It doesn't automatically reduce VM counts or replace backups. IAM and immutability are complementary, not the same thing.

questions.question: A cloud provider's 99.95% monthly availability SLA permits roughly what monthly downtime?
questions.answer: About 22 minutes
questions.explanation: 43,200 min × 0.0005 ≈ 21.6 min/month. 2 min would be ~99.995%, 44 h would be ~93.9%, and 45 s is way too strict.

questions.question: A Service Mesh like Istio typically provides:
options: Traffic management, mTLS, and observability between microservices via sidecar proxies
A container orchestration platform replacing Kubernetes
questions.answer: Traffic management, mTLS, and observability between microservices via sidecar proxies
questions.explanation: Service meshes run L7 proxies (Envoy) per workload to add cross-cutting concerns without app changes. They don't replace databases or orchestrators. Hardware encryption is unrelated.

questions.question: GCP Spanner achieves globally distributed strong consistency by:
options: Using TrueTime (hardware-assisted tightly bounded clocks) with Paxos groups to commit transactions globally
Running a single primary server worldwide
questions.answer: Using TrueTime (hardware-assisted tightly bounded clocks) with Paxos groups to commit transactions globally
questions.explanation: Spanner combines atomic clocks/GPS with Paxos replication and a commit-wait, achieving external consistency. A single primary worldwide would not survive regional failures. Spanner isn't eventually consistent.

questions.question: A Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler scales based on:
options: Observed metrics like CPU utilization or custom metrics against target thresholds
The number of Pods listed in kubectl
The wall-clock hour of the day automatically
questions.answer: Observed metrics like CPU utilization or custom metrics against target thresholds
questions.explanation: HPA consults metrics server/Prometheus adapter and adjusts replicas. It is not purely time-based or latency-only, and kubectl counts are just reads.

questions.question: In FinOps, a common cost-optimization technique for steady workloads is:
options: Reserved Instances or Savings Plans, committing to capacity for 1–3 years at a discount
questions.answer: Reserved Instances or Savings Plans, committing to capacity for 1–3 years at a discount
questions.explanation: RIs/Savings Plans suit predictable baseline load. Burstable is for spiky workloads. Single-AZ hurts reliability. Disabling monitoring saves tiny amounts but impairs ops.

questions.question: In the CAP theorem, a system labeled 'CP' typically:
options: Prioritizes consistency during partitions at the cost of availability (requests may fail)
Allows both consistency and availability with no trade-off
questions.answer: Prioritizes consistency during partitions at the cost of availability (requests may fail)
questions.explanation: CP systems (e.g., HBase, etcd, ZK) refuse service rather than diverge. AP systems (Dynamo, Cassandra default) answer but may return stale data. Zero latency and 'both at once during partition' are myths.

questions.question: An eventually consistent object store experiences read-after-write anomalies mostly because:
options: The request may be routed to a replica that has not yet received the most recent write
questions.answer: The request may be routed to a replica that has not yet received the most recent write
questions.explanation: Replicas converge after propagation; until then a client can read stale data. TCP/DNS/clock issues don't directly cause this anomaly.

questions.question: A Kubernetes cluster experiences OOMKills for a container with request=200Mi and limit=256Mi. Which of the following is the likely root cause and fix?
options: The JVM/runtime sees the full node memory and allocates beyond the cgroup limit; set container-aware memory settings (e.g., -Xmx) or raise the limit
questions.answer: The JVM/runtime sees the full node memory and allocates beyond the cgroup limit; set container-aware memory settings (e.g., -Xmx) or raise the limit
questions.explanation: Many runtimes ignore cgroup limits by default; explicit memory flags (or modern JVMs with MaxRAMPercentage) respect the container limit. Upgrading kernels, tuning CPU, or disabling OOM is not the right fix.

questions.question: Amazon S3 provides which consistency model (since 2020)?
options: Strong read-after-write and list consistency for all objects
Strong only within a single AZ
questions.answer: Strong read-after-write and list consistency for all objects
questions.explanation: AWS changed S3 in 2020 to strong read-after-write and list-after-write globally. The older 'eventual for overwrites' doc is outdated. Single-AZ-only or no guarantees are wrong.

questions.question: A multi-region active-active stateful service that must survive a full regional failure typically relies on:
options: Asynchronous replication with conflict-aware merges (CRDTs or application-level resolution) or globally consistent databases
A single writer replicated synchronously between regions with guaranteed zero RPO
DNS failover with in-region caches only
Cold backups copied once a day
questions.answer: Asynchronous replication with conflict-aware merges (CRDTs or application-level resolution) or globally consistent databases
questions.explanation: Long-distance synchronous replication is rarely practical; async plus reconciliation or products like Spanner/CockroachDB handle this. DNS failover with in-region caches doesn't provide multi-region data durability, and daily cold backups give hours of RPO.

questions.question: The main security implication of 'principle of least privilege' in cloud IAM is:
options: Grant only the permissions needed for a task and scope them to specific resources to limit blast radius on compromise
Give every user administrator access for operational ease
Disable logging to reduce data exposure
questions.answer: Grant only the permissions needed for a task and scope them to specific resources to limit blast radius on compromise
questions.explanation: Least privilege via scoped IAM policies/roles limits damage from credential leaks. Admin-by-default defeats it, static keys are a risk multiplier, and disabling logs hinders forensics.

questions.question: A Kubernetes StatefulSet differs from a Deployment mainly in that it:
options: Maintains stable network identities and stable persistent storage per replica, with ordered rollouts and scaling
Always runs on a single node
questions.answer: Maintains stable network identities and stable persistent storage per replica, with ordered rollouts and scaling
questions.explanation: StatefulSets give each pod a stable hostname (pod-0, pod-1) and its own PVC. Deployments don't offer stable identity, StatefulSets still have replicas and can span nodes.

questions.question: When implementing a serverless application behind an API Gateway, cold start latency can be reduced by:
options: Using provisioned concurrency or smaller runtimes with lightweight init code
Increasing the function timeout to 15 minutes
Using a larger VPC CIDR range
Moving code into a Docker image layer
questions.answer: Using provisioned concurrency or smaller runtimes with lightweight init code
questions.explanation: Provisioned concurrency keeps warm instances; minimizing init time (deps, heavy wire-up) reduces cold starts. Timeouts, CIDR size, and Docker layering do not reduce cold start latency.

questions.question: Network egress bandwidth from one cloud region to another is typically:
options: Chargeable per GB and can be a significant portion of the monthly bill for data-intensive workloads
Charged only for the first GB
Unaffected by cross-AZ vs cross-region boundaries
questions.answer: Chargeable per GB and can be a significant portion of the monthly bill for data-intensive workloads
questions.explanation: Cross-region and internet egress are revenue-generating lines in cloud billing; architects often minimize inter-region traffic. Intra-AZ is typically free or cheap; cross-AZ and cross-region differ.

questions.question: A Kubernetes NetworkPolicy controls:
options: Pod-to-pod and pod-to-external traffic via selectors, using a CNI plugin that enforces the policy
Physical switch ACLs on the datacenter fabric
BGP sessions with the upstream ISP
Kernel firewall on the host outside the cluster
questions.answer: Pod-to-pod and pod-to-external traffic via selectors, using a CNI plugin that enforces the policy
questions.explanation: NetworkPolicy declarations are enforced by CNI plugins (Calico, Cilium) via iptables/eBPF. Fabric ACLs, BGP, and host-global firewalls are operated by infrastructure, not the NetworkPolicy object itself.

questions.question: Which property of a cryptographic hash function means it is infeasible to find any two inputs producing the same digest?
questions.answer: Collision resistance
questions.explanation: Collision resistance protects against finding any two distinct inputs with the same hash. Preimage resistance targets recovering an input from a digest, second-preimage fixes one input and searches for another with the same digest, and the avalanche effect describes large output changes from small input changes.

questions.question: Which cipher category uses the same key for encryption and decryption?
questions.answer: Symmetric-key
questions.explanation: Symmetric algorithms like AES share one secret key for both operations. Asymmetric algorithms use a public/private key pair, homomorphic schemes allow computation on ciphertexts, and quantum-resistant is a design property, not a key model.

questions.question: What is the block size of AES regardless of key length?
questions.answer: 128 bits
questions.explanation: AES always processes 128-bit blocks; 128/192/256 refer only to key sizes. DES and 3DES use 64-bit blocks, but AES was standardized with a fixed 128-bit block.

questions.question: Which component verifies that a TLS server certificate was issued by a trusted authority?
options: Certificate chain validation against root CAs
questions.answer: Certificate chain validation against root CAs
questions.explanation: Clients build a chain from the server cert through intermediates to a trusted root in the local store. DH establishes session keys, HMAC covers handshake integrity, and TCP sequence numbers have no role in authentication.

questions.question: Which firewall type tracks the state of TCP connections and allows return traffic for established sessions?
questions.answer: Stateful firewall
questions.explanation: Stateful firewalls keep a connection table keyed on 5-tuple plus flags. Stateless filters evaluate each packet independently, proxies terminate and re-originate sessions, and 'broadcast firewall' is not a recognized category.

questions.question: Which security device passively monitors traffic and raises alerts without blocking by default?
questions.answer: IDS
questions.explanation: An intrusion detection system observes and alerts. An IPS sits inline and drops matching traffic, NAT translates addresses, and load balancers distribute flows.

questions.question: What does a digital signature primarily provide?
options: Authentication and integrity via the signer's private key
questions.answer: Authentication and integrity via the signer's private key
questions.explanation: A signature is computed with the signer's private key over a message hash and verified with the public key, binding the content to the signer. Encryption provides confidentiality, compression is unrelated, and PFS is a property of key exchange, not signatures.

questions.question: Which protocol encrypts IP packets end-to-end and operates in either transport or tunnel mode?
questions.answer: IPSec
questions.explanation: IPSec's AH and ESP protocols operate at the network layer in transport (protecting the payload) or tunnel (encapsulating the whole IP packet) mode. SSH and TLS work at higher layers, and DNSSEC only signs DNS records.

questions.question: Which attack floods a target from many compromised hosts simultaneously?
questions.answer: DDoS
questions.explanation: A Distributed Denial of Service attack uses a botnet or reflectors across many sources. A plain DoS is single-sourced, MITM intercepts traffic, and replay re-sends captured messages.

questions.question: Which HTTP header instructs browsers to access a domain only over HTTPS for a specified duration?
questions.answer: Strict-Transport-Security
questions.explanation: HSTS pins the client to HTTPS via a max-age directive. X-Frame-Options controls framing, CSP restricts content sources, and X-Content-Type-Options disables MIME sniffing.

questions.question: Which VPN type connects two office networks as if they were one LAN?
questions.answer: Site-to-site VPN
questions.explanation: Site-to-site VPNs (often IPSec) connect gateways between networks. Remote access connects individual clients, SSL-VPN portals serve web-based per-app access, and split tunneling is an option describing which traffic traverses the tunnel.

questions.question: Which organization typically issues trusted X.509 certificates used by browsers?
questions.answer: Certificate Authority (CA)
questions.explanation: CAs sign certificates and are trusted by operating systems and browsers. IANA manages number resources, ICANN oversees DNS policy, and ISOC is an advocacy body.

questions.question: Which attack poisons a local network by associating the attacker's MAC with another host's IP?
questions.answer: ARP spoofing
questions.explanation: ARP spoofing sends forged ARP replies so victims send traffic to the attacker. SYN floods exhaust TCP half-open tables, Smurf uses ICMP amplification, and Teardrop exploits fragmentation reassembly.

questions.question: Which principle states that users should have only the access rights necessary to perform their duties?
questions.answer: Least privilege
questions.explanation: Least privilege minimizes blast radius when an account is compromised. Defense in depth layers controls, separation of duties splits sensitive tasks, and fail-safe defaults deny by default.

questions.question: Which protocol provides authenticated, encrypted remote shell access replacing Telnet?
questions.answer: SSH
questions.explanation: SSH uses public-key or password auth with symmetric encryption over TCP/22. rlogin is insecure, RDP is Microsoft's graphical remote-desktop protocol, and FTP is for file transfer (cleartext by default).

questions.question: Why is AES-ECB generally considered insecure for encrypting images or structured data?
options: Identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, leaking patterns
Its block size is only 64 bits
questions.answer: Identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, leaking patterns
questions.explanation: ECB encrypts each block independently, so repeated plaintext blocks expose structure (the classic 'ECB penguin'). AES's S-box is strong, the block size is 128 bits, and key expansion is defined in the standard.

questions.question: Which AES mode provides authenticated encryption with associated data (AEAD) in a single pass?
questions.answer: GCM
questions.explanation: GCM combines CTR-mode encryption with GHASH for authentication, producing ciphertext and a 128-bit tag. CBC, CTR, and OFB are confidentiality-only modes and require a separate MAC for integrity.

questions.question: Approximately which RSA key size offers comparable strength to a 256-bit ECC key?
questions.answer: 15360 bits
questions.explanation: NIST SP 800-57 equates a 256-bit ECC key with roughly a 15360-bit RSA key (128-bit symmetric equivalent is ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072; 256-bit symmetric ≈ ECC-512 ≈ RSA-15360). 2048-bit RSA is ~112 bits, 3072 is ~128 bits, and 7680 is ~192 bits.

questions.question: Which property does ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (DHE/ECDHE) add to a TLS handshake?
questions.answer: Perfect Forward Secrecy
questions.explanation: Fresh ephemeral keys per session mean that compromise of the long-term private key does not decrypt past captured traffic. Certificates are unchanged, mutual auth is separate, and downgrade protection relies on other mechanisms like signaling cipher suite values.

questions.question: How many round trips does a full TLS 1.3 handshake require before application data can flow?
questions.answer: 1-RTT
questions.explanation: TLS 1.3 completes the handshake in one round trip (ClientHello+key_share → ServerHello+Certificate+Finished, then client Finished can be sent alongside data). 0-RTT is a resumption-only optimization with replay caveats; 2-RTT was TLS 1.2's full handshake.

questions.question: Which method lets a TLS server provide OCSP proof inline instead of the client contacting the CA directly?
questions.answer: OCSP stapling
questions.explanation: Stapling (TLS Certificate Status Request) lets servers fetch a time-limited signed OCSP response and attach it during the handshake, avoiding extra client lookups. CRLs are revocation lists, pinning ties a cert to a host, and SNI routes to the right virtual host.

questions.question: Which IPSec header provides both encryption and authentication of the payload?
questions.answer: ESP
questions.explanation: ESP (protocol 50) encrypts the payload and, with authentication enabled, provides integrity over the ESP packet. AH (protocol 51) authenticates but does not encrypt; IKE/ISAKMP negotiate the SAs.

questions.question: Which IPSec mode encapsulates the entire original IP packet inside a new outer IP header?
questions.answer: Tunnel mode
questions.explanation: Tunnel mode is used between gateways (site-to-site VPNs) and hides the original addresses. Transport mode protects only the payload and reuses the original header; Main/Aggressive are IKEv1 phase-1 modes.

questions.question: Which field uniquely identifies an IPSec Security Association at a receiver?
questions.answer: SPI
questions.explanation: The Security Parameter Index (32 bits) in AH/ESP, combined with destination IP and protocol, identifies the SA. 'SA ID' is informal, 'Tunnel ID' is GRE terminology, and flow label is an IPv6 QoS field.

questions.question: Kerberos clients obtain a service ticket from which component?
questions.answer: Ticket Granting Server (TGS)
questions.explanation: The AS issues a Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT); the client presents the TGT to the TGS to get a service-specific ticket. The KDC is the logical container of AS+TGS, and service principals are the targets, not issuers.

questions.question: What does an HMAC add on top of a plain hash for message authentication?
options: A shared secret key mixed via inner/outer padding
questions.answer: A shared secret key mixed via inner/outer padding
questions.explanation: HMAC = H((K⊕opad) ‖ H((K⊕ipad) ‖ m)), which provides keyed integrity and protects against length-extension attacks that affect plain H(K‖m). It is symmetric (no signatures) and unrelated to compression or PFS.

questions.question: Which DNS amplification technique is commonly abused for reflection DDoS?
options: Recursive servers answering large ANY queries to spoofed sources
Sending TCP FIN to authoritative servers
Poisoning resolver caches with large NXDOMAIN responses
questions.answer: Recursive servers answering large ANY queries to spoofed sources
questions.explanation: Small UDP queries (e.g., ANY/DNSKEY) yield large responses; spoofing the source IP directs the amplified traffic at the victim. FIN is TCP-only, cache poisoning is integrity-focused, and TSIG is for zone-transfer auth.

questions.question: Which Kaminsky-class attack targets weak DNS resolvers by racing forged replies with guessed transaction IDs?
questions.answer: DNS cache poisoning
questions.explanation: Attackers flood spoofed responses while querying, trying to beat the legitimate answer with a matching TXID and source port. DNS tunneling hides data in queries, DNSSEC downgrades strip validation, and NXDOMAIN hijack is a different class.

questions.question: Which type of firewall inspects application-layer content and can terminate/re-originate connections?
questions.answer: Application-layer (proxy) firewall
questions.explanation: Proxy firewalls parse protocols (HTTP, SMTP) and break the direct connection between client and server. Stateless and stateful firewalls act on packet/flow state, and circuit-level gateways only validate session setup (e.g., SOCKS).

questions.question: Which OAuth 2.0 grant type is recommended for single-page applications today?
questions.answer: Authorization code with PKCE
questions.explanation: PKCE binds the authorization request to the token exchange via a code_challenge/verifier, mitigating code interception for public clients. Implicit is deprecated, password grant should be avoided, and client credentials is for machine-to-machine (no user).

questions.question: What does OpenID Connect add on top of OAuth 2.0?
options: An identity layer with ID tokens (JWTs)
questions.answer: An identity layer with ID tokens (JWTs)
questions.explanation: OIDC defines the id_token (a signed JWT describing the end user), a UserInfo endpoint, and discovery. Scopes, refresh tokens, and device flow all exist in OAuth 2.0 itself.

questions.question: Which SAML artifact carries authentication statements from the IdP to the SP?
questions.answer: SAML Assertion
questions.explanation: The Assertion XML (often signed) contains AuthnStatement/AttributeStatement. Metadata describes endpoints and keys, profiles combine assertions/protocols/bindings, and bindings (HTTP-POST, HTTP-Redirect) are transport choices.

questions.question: Which property is NOT offered by WPA2 with CCMP?
options: Forward secrecy between sessions with a static PSK
questions.answer: Forward secrecy between sessions with a static PSK
questions.explanation: WPA2-PSK derives PTKs from the same PMK, so recorded traffic can be decrypted if the PSK leaks (no PFS). WPA3-SAE addresses this. CCMP provides AES-CTR confidentiality, CBC-MAC integrity, and packet-number-based replay protection.

questions.question: Which hash algorithm is considered broken for collision resistance and should be avoided for digital signatures?
questions.answer: SHA-1
questions.explanation: SHAttered (2017) demonstrated a practical SHA-1 collision; major browsers and CAs have since deprecated SHA-1 certificates. SHA-256, SHA-3, and BLAKE2 remain collision-resistant.

questions.question: Which X.509 extension lists additional hostnames a certificate is valid for?
questions.answer: Subject Alternative Name (SAN)
questions.explanation: Modern browsers validate only SAN entries, not the CN. AKI links to the issuer's public key, Basic Constraints distinguishes CA vs end-entity, and Key Usage restricts operations (digitalSignature, keyEncipherment, etc.).

questions.question: What is the main advantage of OCSP over CRL distribution?
options: Eliminates the need for a trusted CA
questions.answer: Provides near real-time per-certificate status
questions.explanation: OCSP answers a single 'is this serial revoked?' query, avoiding large CRL downloads and giving fresher answers. It still relies on CAs/responders and uses digital signatures (asymmetric).

questions.question: Which attack downgrades a TLS client/server to a weaker ciphersuite or protocol version?
questions.answer: POODLE/downgrade attack
questions.explanation: POODLE exploited browsers' fallback to SSL 3.0 CBC padding oracle. BEAST attacks CBC in TLS 1.0, Heartbleed was a buffer over-read in OpenSSL, and Logjam downgraded DH to export-grade primes.

questions.question: Zero Trust architecture replaces implicit network-based trust with which primary model?
questions.answer: Per-request, identity-and-context-aware access decisions
questions.explanation: Zero Trust (e.g., Google BeyondCorp) authenticates the user/device and authorizes each request based on identity, device posture, and context. Perimeter firewalls and ubiquitous VPNs are the legacy model; air-gapping is a different threat model.

questions.question: Which protocol allows a resolver to validate DNS responses against cryptographic signatures?
questions.answer: DNSSEC
questions.explanation: DNSSEC signs records with RRSIG/DNSKEY and chains trust via DS records. DoH (over HTTPS) and DoT (over TLS) encrypt queries for confidentiality but do not themselves authenticate records; mDNS is link-local name resolution.

questions.question: A SYN flood primarily exhausts which resource on the victim?
options: Half-open TCP connection state in the backlog queue
questions.answer: Half-open TCP connection state in the backlog queue
questions.explanation: Attackers send SYNs without completing the handshake, consuming kernel SYN-queue slots. SYN cookies mitigate this by encoding state in the ISN. TLS, disk, and broadcast traffic are unrelated.

questions.question: Which cryptographic flaw enabled the Logjam attack on TLS Diffie-Hellman?
options: Weak RSA padding in PKCS#1 v1.5
Use of 512-bit export-grade DH primes that could be precomputed
questions.answer: Use of 512-bit export-grade DH primes that could be precomputed
questions.explanation: Logjam downgraded connections to DHE_EXPORT, letting attackers break the small shared prime via precomputed number-field sieve work. The other options describe different vulnerabilities (Bleichenbacher, nonce-reuse, truncation).

questions.question: Why is TLS 1.3 0-RTT data vulnerable to replay, and what is the typical mitigation?
options: It uses weak ciphers; upgrade to AES-GCM
Early data reuses a PSK-derived key with no freshness exchange; only idempotent requests should use 0-RTT or servers must track replay
The ServerHello is unencrypted; pin the certificate
Signature algorithms are optional; require ECDSA
questions.answer: Early data reuses a PSK-derived key with no freshness exchange; only idempotent requests should use 0-RTT or servers must track replay
questions.explanation: 0-RTT sends application data before the server's first message, so an attacker can replay captured ciphertext to the same or another server in the cluster. Mitigations: restrict 0-RTT to idempotent methods and maintain single-use tickets. The other items describe unrelated properties.

questions.question: Which BGP mitigation technique uses RPKI to reject route announcements not originated by the legitimate ASN?
questions.answer: Route Origin Validation (ROV) with ROAs
questions.explanation: ROAs cryptographically bind a prefix to an origin ASN; ROV marks announcements as valid/invalid/unknown. BGPsec signs the AS_PATH (not widely deployed), MD5 protects the TCP session, and max-prefix limits cap announcement counts.

questions.question: In IKEv2, what replaces IKEv1's separate Main/Aggressive and Quick modes?
options: A single IKE_SA_INIT exchange followed by IKE_AUTH, establishing both IKE SA and the first child SA
questions.answer: A single IKE_SA_INIT exchange followed by IKE_AUTH, establishing both IKE SA and the first child SA
questions.explanation: IKEv2 consolidates phase 1 and phase 2 into IKE_SA_INIT (DH, nonces, SA proposals) and IKE_AUTH (identity, certs, first child SA). Aggressive/Main/Quick modes are IKEv1 constructs; XAUTH was an IKEv1 extension.

questions.question: Which attack recovered WEP keys by collecting IVs and exploiting RC4 weak keys?
questions.answer: FMS/PTW attack
questions.explanation: Fluhrer-Mantin-Shamir (2001) and the improved PTW attack recover the WEP key after capturing modest IV traffic. KRACK reinstalls WPA2 nonces, Bleichenbacher targets RSA PKCS#1 v1.5, and DROWN exploits SSLv2.

questions.question: Which amplification factor class is typical for memcached-based reflection DDoS?
questions.answer: Tens of thousands (extreme)
questions.explanation: UDP memcached (port 11211) could respond with multi-megabyte data to tiny requests, yielding amplification factors of tens of thousands — as seen in the 2018 GitHub 1.35 Tbps attack. DNS is ~50x, NTP monlist ~500x, SSDP ~30x.

questions.question: Which side-channel attack broke AES-NI-less software implementations by measuring cache timing of table lookups?
questions.answer: Bernstein's cache-timing attack
questions.explanation: Bernstein (2005) recovered AES keys from OpenSSL by correlating cache-miss timing with T-table access patterns. Spectre/Meltdown are speculative execution issues, Rowhammer flips DRAM bits, and fault injection requires physical access.

questions.question: In a Kerberos AS-REP roasting attack, what exact weakness is exploited?
options: Accounts with 'do not require pre-authentication' return ciphertext encrypted with a key derived from the user's password, enabling offline cracking
The TGS blindly signs any SPN request
Service tickets are issued without an authenticator
Inter-realm trust tickets bypass the KDC
questions.answer: Accounts with 'do not require pre-authentication' return ciphertext encrypted with a key derived from the user's password, enabling offline cracking
questions.explanation: When PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP is disabled, any attacker can request an AS-REP and crack it offline because part is encrypted with the user's long-term key. The other statements misrepresent Kerberos; Kerberoasting (a different attack) targets service-ticket encryption.

questions.question: Which TLS 1.3 feature prevents middleboxes from tampering with or downgrading handshake parameters?
options: Transcript hash covered by the Finished MAC and signature_algorithms extension
questions.answer: Transcript hash covered by the Finished MAC and signature_algorithms extension
questions.explanation: The Finished messages authenticate the entire handshake transcript with keys bound to the negotiated parameters, so any modification is detected. Resumption is an optimization, ALPN selects the application protocol, and ESNI/ECH hide the server name.

questions.question: What does BGPsec provide beyond RPKI origin validation?
options: Cryptographic signing of each AS in the AS_PATH so the entire path is verifiable
Replacement of TCP as the BGP transport
questions.answer: Cryptographic signing of each AS in the AS_PATH so the entire path is verifiable
questions.explanation: BGPsec (RFC 8205) has each transit AS sign the update, defeating path manipulation that ROV alone cannot catch. It does not encrypt UPDATEs, does not automatically blackhole, and still runs over TCP.

questions.question: A 1 Gbps link with one-way propagation delay 20 ms carries TCP traffic. What is the bandwidth-delay product, and what does it imply for throughput?
options: ~2.5 MB; you need a receive window of at least that size to saturate the link
questions.answer: ~2.5 MB; you need a receive window of at least that size to saturate the link
questions.explanation: BDP = 1 Gbps × 20 ms × 2 (RTT) ≈ 5 × 10^6 bytes one-way or 5 MB RTT — commonly quoted as ~2.5 MB one-way in flight. To keep the pipe full TCP must advertise a window at least that large (window scaling required since classic 16-bit windows cap at 64 KB). Fragmentation and MTU are separate concerns.

questions.question: With a generator polynomial of degree r, how many bit errors are guaranteed to be detected by a CRC?
options: All burst errors of length ≤ r, plus all odd-numbered errors if (x+1) is a factor
All errors up to r/2 errors in a block
questions.answer: All burst errors of length ≤ r, plus all odd-numbered errors if (x+1) is a factor
questions.explanation: A CRC with an r-degree generator detects any burst ≤ r, detects bursts of length r+1 with probability 1−2^(1−r), and detects all odd-bit errors iff (x+1) divides the generator. The other options misstate the guarantee.

questions.question: Which routing metric does OSPF use by default on Cisco implementations?
options: Composite of bandwidth, delay, load, and reliability
questions.answer: Bandwidth-based cost (10^8 / interface bandwidth)
questions.explanation: OSPF cost is inversely proportional to interface bandwidth with a reference of 100 Mbps by default (tunable with auto-cost reference-bandwidth). Hop count is RIP's metric, pure delay is not OSPF, and the composite metric is EIGRP's.

questions.question: Under TCP Reno with a loss probability p, the steady-state throughput is approximately:
questions.explanation: The Mathis et al. model gives throughput ≈ MSS · √(3/2) / (RTT · √p), reflecting AIMD's sawtooth recovery after each loss event. The other formulas do not match the AIMD steady state.

questions.question: Which HTTP/2 feature directly causes the HOL-blocking problem that motivated HTTP/3?
options: Multiplexed streams carried over a single TCP connection
questions.answer: Multiplexed streams carried over a single TCP connection
questions.explanation: A single lost TCP segment stalls all multiplexed HTTP/2 streams until retransmission. HTTP/3 moves to QUIC (over UDP), where each stream has its own reliability and HOL-blocking is eliminated across streams. HPACK, push, and priorities are unrelated to transport HOL.

questions.question: Which 802.11 mechanism reduces collisions from hidden terminals?
questions.answer: RTS/CTS handshake
questions.explanation: An RTS announces an intended transmission; the AP's CTS is heard by hidden nodes that could not hear the sender, causing them to defer. Backoff addresses general contention, beacons advertise APs, and association is for joining the BSS.

questions.question: Why does WPA2's 4-way handshake remain vulnerable to the KRACK attack even with strong passwords?
options: The PSK can be reversed from the 4-way handshake capture
Message 3 can be replayed, causing the client to reinstall an already-used PTK and reset nonces/replay counters
AES-CCMP uses a 64-bit block that is too small
The anonce is predictable from the BSSID
questions.answer: Message 3 can be replayed, causing the client to reinstall an already-used PTK and reset nonces/replay counters
questions.explanation: KRACK (2017) forces nonce reuse by replaying msg 3 of the handshake, enabling decryption or forgery regardless of password strength. The PSK is not directly recoverable, CCMP uses AES-128, and the anonce is random.

questions.question: Which 5G feature allows multiple logical networks with isolated SLAs to share the same physical infrastructure?
questions.answer: Network slicing
questions.explanation: Slicing creates end-to-end logical networks (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC) with dedicated policies over shared RAN/core via NFV. CA bonds carriers for bandwidth, dual connectivity combines two radio legs, and CoMP is cooperative multipoint transmission.

questions.question: Which SDN plane is responsible for forwarding packets at line rate on switches?
questions.answer: Data plane
questions.explanation: In SDN, the data (forwarding) plane executes match/action rules on each packet. The control plane computes rules (e.g., an OpenFlow controller), the management plane handles configuration/telemetry, and the application plane hosts network apps above the controller.

questions.question: Which protocol is the canonical southbound API between an SDN controller and switches?
questions.answer: OpenFlow
questions.explanation: OpenFlow standardizes flow-table programming and packet-in/packet-out messages. NETCONF configures devices, BGP is a routing protocol, and SNMP is for monitoring.

questions.question: What does NFV stand for?
questions.answer: Network Function Virtualization
questions.explanation: NFV decouples network functions (firewalls, load balancers, routers) from proprietary hardware onto VMs/containers. The other expansions are not standard.

questions.question: Which three 5G service categories are defined by ITU IMT-2020?
questions.answer: eMBB, URLLC, mMTC
questions.explanation: enhanced Mobile Broadband, Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications, and massive Machine-Type Communications are the headline 5G categories. The other groupings are duplex modes, traffic classes, or applications.

questions.question: Which IoT messaging protocol is publish-subscribe over TCP and widely used in constrained environments?
questions.answer: MQTT
questions.explanation: MQTT uses a broker and topic-based pub/sub over TCP/8883 (TLS). CoAP mimics REST over UDP, AMQP is heavier and queue-centric, and XMPP is an XML messaging protocol.

questions.question: Which transport protocol does CoAP run over by default?
questions.answer: UDP
questions.explanation: CoAP was designed for constrained devices and uses UDP (often with DTLS). TCP, SCTP, and QUIC bindings exist as later additions but are not the default.

questions.question: Which type of computing pushes processing close to end-users and IoT devices to reduce latency?
questions.answer: Edge computing
questions.explanation: Edge computing runs workloads at the network edge (base stations, gateways) for sub-10-ms latency. Cloud is centralized, mainframes are monolithic big-iron, and batch is a processing model.

questions.question: Which modern application-layer protocol runs on top of QUIC?
questions.answer: HTTP/3
questions.explanation: HTTP/3 is defined over QUIC (UDP+TLS 1.3+streams). HTTP/1.1 runs on TCP, SMTP/FTP over TCP, and none of them normally use QUIC.

questions.question: Which satellite constellation class is used by Starlink and OneWeb?
questions.answer: LEO
questions.explanation: Starlink and OneWeb orbit at ~550–1200 km (low Earth orbit) to reduce latency to ~20–40 ms. GEO is ~36,000 km (high latency), MEO includes GPS/Galileo, and HEO (Highly Elliptical) is used by Molniya/Tundra.

questions.question: Which Wi-Fi generation operates in the 6 GHz band and was branded 'Wi-Fi 6E'?
questions.answer: 802.11ax operating in 6 GHz
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi 6E is 802.11ax extended into the newly opened 6 GHz spectrum. 802.11ac is Wi-Fi 5 (5 GHz), 802.11be is Wi-Fi 7, and 802.11n is Wi-Fi 4.

questions.question: Which IPv6 transition mechanism allows IPv6 traffic to tunnel over an IPv4 network using 2002::/16 prefixes?
questions.answer: 6to4
questions.explanation: 6to4 embeds the IPv4 address in the 2002:: prefix for automatic tunneling. Teredo tunnels IPv6 over UDP (for clients behind NAT), NAT64 translates between IPv6 and IPv4, and dual stack runs both natively.

questions.question: Which MANO component orchestrates VNFs in the NFV architecture?
questions.answer: NFV Orchestrator (NFVO)
questions.explanation: NFVO manages network services and resource orchestration. The VIM (Virtualized Infrastructure Manager) controls NFVI, the VNFM manages VNF lifecycles, and EMS handles element management.

questions.question: Which core function in 5G's service-based architecture terminates user-plane traffic and provides packet routing?
questions.answer: UPF
questions.explanation: The User Plane Function forwards packets and enforces QoS. AMF handles access and mobility, SMF controls sessions, and PCF owns policy.

questions.question: In IoT MQTT, which QoS level guarantees message delivery exactly once?
questions.answer: QoS 2
questions.explanation: QoS 0 is at-most-once (fire-and-forget), QoS 1 is at-least-once (PUBACK), and QoS 2 is exactly-once using a four-part handshake (PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCOMP). There is no QoS 3.

questions.question: Which high-level language programs the P4 data plane?
questions.answer: P4
questions.explanation: P4 (Programming Protocol-independent Packet Processors) defines parsers, tables, and actions for programmable ASICs. OpenFlow is a control-plane API, YANG models configurations, and Python is general-purpose.

questions.question: Which SDN controller is widely deployed in carrier-grade deployments and uses a YANG-based model with OpenDaylight heritage?
questions.answer: ONOS
questions.explanation: ONOS (Open Network Operating System) targets service providers with high availability and YANG-based NBIs; OpenDaylight is a sibling project with overlapping roots. Ryu, Floodlight, and POX are research/teaching controllers.

questions.question: Which OpenFlow construct specifies how matching packets should be processed?
options: Flow entry containing match fields, priority, counters, and actions
questions.answer: Flow entry containing match fields, priority, counters, and actions
questions.explanation: Each flow entry in an OpenFlow pipeline has match fields, priority, counters, a timeout, and an instruction/action set. Group and meter tables are optional add-ons, and a MAC table is a legacy L2 artifact.

questions.question: Which 5G feature delivers URLLC latency of ~1 ms?
options: Short TTI (mini-slot), grant-free uplink, and edge computing
questions.answer: Short TTI (mini-slot), grant-free uplink, and edge computing
questions.explanation: URLLC uses sub-millisecond mini-slots, pre-scheduled grant-free transmissions, and MEC to cut air-interface plus backhaul latency. Larger aggregations and higher-order modulation raise throughput but don't shrink latency; 2x2 MIMO is too modest for URLLC.

questions.question: Which property distinguishes Segment Routing (SR) from traditional MPLS signaling?
options: SR stores path state in the packet (segment list) with no LDP/RSVP signaling required
SR uses LDP but not BGP
questions.answer: SR stores path state in the packet (segment list) with no LDP/RSVP signaling required
questions.explanation: Segment Routing encodes the path as a stack of segments (node/adjacency SIDs) at the ingress, eliminating per-flow state in the core and removing LDP/RSVP-TE. SR can run with MPLS or IPv6 data planes; it augments, not replaces, IGP.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between SRv6 and SR-MPLS?
options: SRv6 uses 128-bit IPv6 addresses as segment identifiers carried in an IPv6 extension header; SR-MPLS uses MPLS labels
SR-MPLS supports IPv6 while SRv6 does not
questions.answer: SRv6 uses 128-bit IPv6 addresses as segment identifiers carried in an IPv6 extension header; SR-MPLS uses MPLS labels
questions.explanation: SRv6 encodes segments as IPv6 addresses inside an SRH (Segment Routing Header, type 4), while SR-MPLS uses MPLS labels. Both provide the same source-routing semantics, but SRv6 leverages the IPv6 data plane directly.

questions.question: Which MEC concept reuses a subset of a cloud orchestration system to place workloads at the base station?
options: MEC host with MEC platform managing MEC applications
questions.answer: MEC host with MEC platform managing MEC applications
questions.explanation: ETSI MEC defines a MEC host (compute/storage/network) with a MEC platform providing services (RNIS, location) to MEC apps. The other options describe unrelated components.

questions.question: Which characteristic of QUIC reduces connection setup time compared with TCP+TLS 1.3?
options: Combines transport and crypto handshakes into one flight, with 0-RTT on resumption
Uses SCTP streams under the hood
Replaces UDP with a faster datagram protocol
questions.answer: Combines transport and crypto handshakes into one flight, with 0-RTT on resumption
questions.explanation: QUIC merges the TCP and TLS handshakes, saving a round trip on first connection and enabling 0-RTT on resumption. QUIC uses UDP and implements its own loss recovery and congestion control (e.g., Cubic, BBR).

questions.question: Which IoT LPWAN technology uses chirp spread spectrum with class A/B/C device classes?
questions.answer: LoRaWAN
questions.explanation: LoRaWAN uses LoRa's chirp-spread-spectrum PHY in sub-GHz ISM bands. Class A is downlink only after uplink, B has scheduled receive windows, C listens continuously. NB-IoT runs in licensed LTE bands, Sigfox uses UNB, and Zigbee is WPAN.

questions.question: Which automation framework is agentless, uses SSH, and describes state in YAML playbooks?
questions.answer: Ansible
questions.explanation: Ansible runs over SSH and applies YAML playbooks/roles without long-running agents. Puppet and Chef use agents and their own DSLs; Salt uses ZeroMQ with minions by default.

questions.question: Which NETCONF data modeling language is most commonly used for describing device configuration schemas?
questions.answer: YANG
questions.explanation: YANG (RFC 7950) models NETCONF/RESTCONF configuration and state. XML Schema validates XML documents, JSON Schema describes JSON, and Protobuf defines gRPC messages.

questions.question: Which property makes Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) more efficient than Wi-Fi 5 in dense environments?
options: OFDMA with RU allocation and BSS coloring
questions.answer: OFDMA with RU allocation and BSS coloring
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi 6 splits channels into Resource Units with OFDMA so multiple stations share a transmission, and BSS coloring reduces co-channel deferral. It also retains 2.4 GHz and extends MU-MIMO; wider channels existed in Wi-Fi 5.

questions.question: Which TSN (IEEE 802.1) feature provides deterministic low-latency scheduling for Ethernet?
questions.answer: 802.1Qbv time-aware shaper
questions.explanation: 802.1Qbv uses a gate control list synchronized by 802.1AS (PTP) to schedule transmissions in tight time windows. STP is loop avoidance, PoE delivers power, and 802.1X handles authentication.

questions.question: DetNet differs from TSN primarily by:
options: Operating at Layer 3 across routed networks, not just a bridged Ethernet domain
questions.answer: Operating at Layer 3 across routed networks, not just a bridged Ethernet domain
questions.explanation: DetNet (RFC 8655) extends deterministic forwarding across IP/MPLS networks, whereas TSN targets Ethernet bridges. DetNet can leverage MPLS or IP forwarding planes and supports both IPv4 and IPv6.

questions.question: Which SDN architecture layer exposes REST APIs to network-facing applications?
questions.answer: Northbound interface of the controller
questions.explanation: Northbound APIs (REST/gRPC) let applications program the controller. Southbound talks to switches, east-west federates controllers, and the management plane handles operations.

questions.question: Which feature of 5G NR supports both FR1 (sub-6 GHz) and FR2 (mmWave) bands with flexible numerologies?
options: Fixed 15 kHz SCS inherited from LTE
questions.answer: Scalable subcarrier spacing (15/30/60/120/240 kHz)
questions.explanation: 5G NR defines multiple numerologies via SCS µ, enabling short symbols at mmWave and longer symbols at sub-6 GHz. LTE was fixed at 15 kHz, CDMA is 3G, and HARQ remains in NR.

questions.question: Which storage primitive supports object versioning and eventual consistency across regions in modern cloud systems?
questions.answer: Object storage (S3-style)
questions.explanation: Object stores keep immutable versions keyed by object name, replicate across regions, and expose HTTP APIs. Block and file storage are region-local with different consistency models, and RAM disks are ephemeral.

questions.question: Which protocol replaces traditional hardware middleboxes by chaining VNFs with NSH (Network Service Header)?
questions.answer: Service Function Chaining (SFC)
questions.explanation: SFC steers flows through an ordered list of VNFs using NSH to carry metadata and path identifiers. LACP bundles links, BFD detects failures, and PIM-SM is a multicast routing protocol.

questions.question: Which intent-based networking (IBN) component translates business policy into device configuration?
questions.answer: Intent translator / policy compiler
questions.explanation: IBN systems (Cisco DNA Center, Juniper Apstra) compile high-level intent into device-specific config and continuously validate. CLI, BGP policy, and TACACS+ are supporting tools, not the translation engine.

questions.question: Which advantage does eBPF bring to Linux-based SDN data planes?
options: In-kernel programmable packet processing without kernel modifications
questions.answer: In-kernel programmable packet processing without kernel modifications
questions.explanation: eBPF lets programs run in the kernel safely (verifier + JIT), powering Cilium, Calico, Katran. It does not require hypervisors, runs in kernel space, and can augment (not replace) the TCP stack.

questions.question: Which MQTT feature enables a client to receive messages it missed while disconnected?
questions.answer: Persistent session with QoS ≥ 1 subscriptions
questions.explanation: With CleanSession=false and QoS 1/2, brokers buffer messages for offline clients and redeliver on reconnect. Clean sessions discard state, retained messages only cover the latest on a topic, and LWT publishes on disconnect.

questions.question: Which 5G feature reduces control-plane latency by letting the UE send small data in the RRC_INACTIVE state?
options: Early data transmission (EDT) / small data transmission (SDT)
questions.answer: Early data transmission (EDT) / small data transmission (SDT)
questions.explanation: SDT/EDT allows small payloads without transitioning to CONNECTED, saving signaling overhead for IoT. Full RRC setup and handover are the heavier alternatives, and IMS registration is for voice.

questions.question: What is the role of the AMF in the 5G core?
options: Access and Mobility Management including NAS signaling
questions.answer: Access and Mobility Management including NAS signaling
questions.explanation: The AMF terminates NAS signaling, handles registration/authentication, and tracks UE mobility. UPF forwards user-plane packets, PCF decides policy, and UDM manages subscription data.

questions.question: Which programmable-networking abstraction lets operators express forwarding independently of protocol, validated at compile time?
questions.answer: P4 pipeline with parser/match+action/deparser stages
questions.explanation: P4 programs define how to parse headers, which tables to match, and how to modify/emit packets, compiled to a target's pipeline. Fixed-function ASICs lack programmability, OpenFlow 1.0 has a fixed 12-tuple match, and NETCONF is configuration only.

questions.question: What is the key benefit of OpenFlow 1.3's multiple flow tables (pipeline) over OpenFlow 1.0's single table?
options: Reduces the cross-product explosion of flow entries by decomposing matches across stages
questions.answer: Reduces the cross-product explosion of flow entries by decomposing matches across stages
questions.explanation: Matching on multiple fields in one table requires the cross-product of values; pipelined tables let successive stages match independent fields, drastically reducing entries. Flow entries and packet-in remain, and TLS is optional.

questions.question: Which SRv6 micro-SID compression technique packs multiple segment identifiers into a single 128-bit IPv6 address to reduce header size?
questions.answer: uSID (micro-segment) / G-SRv6
questions.explanation: uSID concatenates several 16/32-bit micro-SIDs into a 128-bit address, cutting the SRH length dramatically. VPNv6, 6PE, and 6VPE are MPLS-based IPv6 VPN mechanisms, unrelated to SRH compression.

questions.question: Which 5G feature supports URLLC redundancy by sending duplicate PDCP packets over two independent paths?
questions.answer: PDCP duplication with multi-connectivity
questions.explanation: 3GPP defined PDCP packet duplication across MCG/SCG (dual connectivity) or carrier-aggregation legs for reliability. HARQ and RLC AM help but are not path-redundant; MCS reduction just lowers throughput.

questions.question: Which QUIC mechanism enables connections to survive client IP changes (e.g., Wi-Fi to cellular handover)?
questions.answer: Connection ID (CID) decoupled from 4-tuple
questions.explanation: QUIC uses a connection ID carried in each packet header, so servers can associate packets with the same connection even after the client's source IP/port changes (connection migration). MPTCP, Mobile IP, and ICMP are unrelated.

questions.question: Which trade-off is inherent to LEO satellite constellations like Starlink?
options: Low latency but requires dense constellations and handovers every few minutes
High latency but minimal satellite count
questions.answer: Low latency but requires dense constellations and handovers every few minutes
questions.explanation: LEO satellites move rapidly overhead, requiring hundreds–thousands of them and constant user-terminal handover, in exchange for tens of ms latency. GEO gives static coverage at 500+ ms, and all constellations still rely on ground stations or inter-satellite links.

questions.question: Which Wi-Fi 7 feature doubles link reliability by transmitting the same frame over multiple bands simultaneously?
questions.answer: Multi-Link Operation (MLO)
questions.explanation: 802.11be's MLO lets a device aggregate 2.4/5/6 GHz links for higher throughput or redundancy. OFDMA/TWT/BSS coloring are Wi-Fi 6 features (retained in Wi-Fi 7 but not unique to it).

questions.question: Which feature of NFV MANO enables dynamic scaling and healing of VNFs based on KPIs?
options: Closed-loop automation with VNFM and NFVO coordination
questions.answer: Closed-loop automation with VNFM and NFVO coordination
questions.explanation: Telemetry triggers the VNFM/NFVO to scale in/out, replace unhealthy instances, or migrate VNFs — the core value of MANO closed loops. Manual CLI, static provisioning, and BGP flowspec do not provide that orchestration.

questions.question: Which property makes DetNet suitable for industrial automation that TCP/IP traditionally cannot guarantee?
options: Bounded worst-case end-to-end latency and near-zero congestion loss via explicit resource reservation
questions.answer: Bounded worst-case end-to-end latency and near-zero congestion loss via explicit resource reservation
questions.explanation: DetNet combines resource reservation, explicit routes, and packet replication/elimination to achieve deterministic delivery required by factory control. Best-effort IP offers no bounds; peak throughput and CPU cost are not the defining goals.

questions.question: Which consequence follows from HTTP/3 using UDP for its transport?
options: Middleboxes doing stateful TCP inspection cannot enforce policy without QUIC-aware parsing
The application layer must re-implement IP routing
questions.answer: Middleboxes doing stateful TCP inspection cannot enforce policy without QUIC-aware parsing
questions.explanation: Many firewalls, WAFs, and load balancers were built around TCP state; QUIC requires them to parse UDP/QUIC or punt. IP routing is unchanged, TLS 1.3 is embedded in QUIC, and QUIC natively multiplexes.

questions.question: Which challenge does network slicing most acutely introduce in a production 5G core?
options: End-to-end SLA enforcement and isolation across RAN, transport, and core under shared resources
Making subscribers provide their own cores
questions.answer: End-to-end SLA enforcement and isolation across RAN, transport, and core under shared resources
questions.explanation: A slice is only as deterministic as its weakest link; enforcing bandwidth, latency, and security across all three domains with shared hardware is the hard operational problem. The other options are not real slicing challenges.

questions.question: Which SDN resilience issue arises when the controller-to-switch control channel is partitioned from the data plane?
options: Switches enter fail-open or fail-closed mode per configuration, losing dynamic policy updates until reconnection
Packets stop forwarding instantly regardless of mode
questions.answer: Switches enter fail-open or fail-closed mode per configuration, losing dynamic policy updates until reconnection
questions.explanation: OpenFlow switches can be configured to fail-secure (drop on timeout) or fail-standalone (use existing rules). In both cases, dynamic reprogramming pauses until the controller is reachable. STP and IP version are unaffected.

questions.question: Which characteristic distinguishes fog computing from cloud and edge computing?
options: A hierarchical compute tier between edge devices and the cloud, with more resources than edge nodes but closer to users than the datacenter
Compute performed only inside the browser
A replacement for all centralized datacenters
questions.answer: A hierarchical compute tier between edge devices and the cloud, with more resources than edge nodes but closer to users than the datacenter
questions.explanation: Fog nodes (e.g., regional gateways, central-office servers) aggregate and process data from many edge devices before forwarding to the cloud, trading some latency for more capacity than pure edge. It is not a synonym for public cloud, not browser-bound, and complements rather than replaces datacenters.
